A rehabilitation engineer follows a four-step **HAAT logic** (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology, from Cook & Hussey):

- **Step 1 — Human:** identify residual abilities (strength, ROM, cognition, sensation).
- **Step 2 — Activity:** identify the goal task (walk, eat, write, communicate).
- **Step 3 — AT:** match to device class.
- **Step 4 — Context:** fit to environment (home, workplace, school, outdoor).

### 1.2.3 Engineering Parameters that Define an Assistive Device

Every assistive device, regardless of class, is evaluated against the following **engineering specifications** (Examiner's favourite question):

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Typical Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static load capacity | $W_{max}$ in N (or kg) | Cane: 113 kg; Crutch: 160 kg; Wheelchair: 113 kg standard, 180 kg bariatric | Maximum user weight the device can bear without yielding |
| Adjustability range | $L_{adj}$ in cm | Cane: 70–95; Crutch: arm-pit: 110–140, forearm: 70–100 | Height window to match user anthropometry |
| Tip diameter / contact area | $d_{tip}$ in mm | 15–40 mm rubber ferrule | Pressure on floor = $W/d_{tip}^2$ — affects slip and floor damage |
| Energy cost reduction | $\Delta \text{ECW}$ in ml O₂/kg·m | 0.05–0.20 reduction with proper aid | Lower is better — quantifies benefit |
| Mechanical Advantage | MA = $F_{out}/F_{in}$ | Wheelchair hand-rim: 1.0–1.5 | How much user input is amplified |
| Cycle / actuation force | $F_{act}$ in N | Hand-rim push: 30–80 N | Force user must apply per stroke |
| Stability angle | $\theta_{tip}$ in degrees | 8°–12° safety margin | Tilt angle before device tips |

### 1.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{ECW} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{v \cdot m}$ | Energy cost of walking; $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ in ml/min, $v$ in m/min, $m$ in kg |
| 2 | $\text{Pace} = \dfrac{1}{\text{stride length}} \cdot 60$ | Steps per minute, normalised gait metric |
| 3 | $\text{Ramp slope ratio} = \dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}$ | ADA safe limit $\leq \dfrac{1}{12}$; Kerala PWD $\leq \dfrac{1}{12}$ |
| 4 | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | Ramp angle $\alpha$ from height $h$ over length $L$ |
| 5 | $F_{required} = mg \sin \alpha + \mu mg \cos \alpha$ | Push force up a ramp, $\mu$ = rolling resistance coefficient |
| 6 | $\text{MA} = \dfrac{F_{load}}{F_{effort}}$ | Mechanical advantage of lever-based aid |
| 7 | $P_{contact} = \dfrac{W_{user}}{A_{tip}}$ | Contact pressure under assistive device tip |
| 8 | $\text{Speed Index} = \dfrac{v_{with\ aid}}{v_{normal}}$ | Performance ratio (≈ 0.5–0.8 for crutches, 0.7–1.0 for cane) |
| 9 | $\text{Cadence ratio} = \dfrac{C_{device}}{C_{normal}}$ | Gait rhythm preservation |
| 10 | $\tau_{stability} = W \cdot \dfrac{b}{2}$ | Restoring torque; $b$ = wheelbase width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are used by KTU examiners in 14-mark Part B questions to test whether a student can **quantify the benefit** of an assistive device — not just describe it. Memorise ECW and ramp-slope relations; they appear almost every KTU cycle.

### 1.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is used by:
- **Biomedical R&D labs** (IIT Madras TTK Center for Rehabilitation Research, NIEPMD Chennai) for designing indigenous assistive devices.
- **Certification agencies** (BIS, ISO 9999:2016 for AT classification) for product approval.
- **Clinicians** (Physiatrists, Occupational Therapists) for prescription writing.
- **Social-sector engineers** for compliance with **RPwD Act 2016 (India)** and **UN CRPD (2006)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 1.3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Cost of Walking (ECW) for a User on Axillary Crutches

**Problem setup:** A 70 kg person with a lower-limb fracture walks on axillary crutches at $v = 30$ m/min with oxygen consumption $\dot{V}_{O_2} = 1500$ ml/min. Compute the ECW and compare to normal walking.

**Step 1 — Definition of ECW**

The energy cost of walking is the volume of oxygen consumed per kilogram of body mass per metre of horizontal distance travelled.

$$
\text{ECW} = \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{m \cdot v}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values**

$$
\dot{V}_{O_2} = 1500 \text{ ml/min}, \quad m = 70 \text{ kg}, \quad v = 30 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
\text{ECW} = \frac{1500 \text{ ml/min}}{70 \text{ kg} \cdot 30 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1500
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 70 \times 30 = 2100
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio**

$$
\text{ECW} = \frac{1500}{2100} = 0.714 \text{ ml O}_2 / \text{kg·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to normal baseline**

Normal adult walking ECW $\approx 0.15$ to $0.20 \text{ ml O}_2/\text{kg·m}$.

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{0.714}{0.18} \approx 3.97
$$

> The crutch user expends roughly **4× the energy** of a normal walker per unit distance. This is why KTU examiner expects you to comment on **why gait-aid prescription must consider cardiovascular fitness**.

**Step 6 — Practical implication**

For a healthy young adult with VO₂max ≈ 50 ml/kg/min, sustainable walking speed on crutches is:

$$
v_{sust} = \frac{\text{VO}_2^{max} \cdot k}{m \cdot \text{ECW}}
$$

where $k \approx 0.5$ is the sustainable fraction. Estimating:

$$
v_{sust} = \frac{50 \times 0.5}{70 \times 0.714} = \frac{25}{49.98} \approx 0.50 \text{ m/s}
$$

which matches the empirical crutch-walking range given earlier.

---

### 1.3.2 Derivation 2 — Wheelchair Push-Force on a Ramp

**Problem setup:** A 80 kg person + 15 kg wheelchair = 95 kg total mass climbs a 1:12 ramp (ADA max). Rolling resistance coefficient for rubber tyre on concrete $\mu_r = 0.015$. Compute the force each hand must apply on the hand-rim (assume both rims used).

**Step 1 — Free-body on the ramp**

Total weight $W = mg = 95 \times 9.81 = 931.95$ N.

**Step 2 — Ramp angle**

Slope ratio 1:12 means $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{1}{12}$.

$$
\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{1}{12}\right) = 4.7636^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Sine and cosine values**

$$
\sin \alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2 + 12^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{145}} = 0.0830
$$

$$
\cos \alpha = \frac{12}{\sqrt{145}} = 0.9965
$$

**Step 4 — Force required to climb**

Total push force on wheels = component along ramp + rolling resistance.

$$
F_{total} = mg \sin \alpha + \mu_r \, mg \cos \alpha
$$

$$
F_{total} = 931.95 \times 0.0830 + 0.015 \times 931.95 \times 0.9965
$$

$$
F_{total} = 77.35 + 13.93 = 91.28 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Distribute to both rims (assuming equal bilateral push)**

$$
F_{each\ rim} = \frac{F_{total}}{2} = \frac{91.28}{2} = 45.64 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify against ergonomics limit**

Recommended sustainable push force on hand-rim = 30–60 N per stroke. The 45.64 N is **within sustainable range** for an adult but **exceeds capacity for an elderly or paediatric user**, justifying powered wheelchair prescription.

---

### 1.3.3 Derivation 3 — Cane Tip Pressure and Floor Safety

**Problem setup:** A 70 kg person (weight 686.7 N) bears 25% body weight on a cane with rubber tip of diameter 25 mm. Compute contact pressure and check against slip/floor limits.

**Step 1 — Load on the cane**

$$
W_{cane} = 0.25 \times 686.7 = 171.68 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Tip contact area**

$$
A_{tip} = \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \left(\frac{0.025}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \times (0.0125)^2
$$

$$
A_{tip} = \pi \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Contact pressure**

$$
P = \frac{W_{cane}}{A_{tip}} = \frac{171.68}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
P = 3.497 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.35 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against floor compressive limit**

- Vinyl flooring: limit ≈ 2.5 MPa (safe)
- Marble / granite: ≈ 100 MPa (safe)
- Soft wooden floor: ≈ 0.6 MPa — marginal; rubber tip acceptable.

> This is the kind of calculation a KTU 14-mark question can require when asking *"justify the choice of tip material in a cane for an elderly user on a wooden floor."*

---

### 1.3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Mobility Aid

| Patient Profile | Recommended Aid | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Hemiplegia (stroke), one weak side | Single-point cane (contralateral) | Reduces hip abductor moment by ~40%; off-loads 25% BW |
| Bilateral lower-limb weakness, poor balance | Wide-base quad cane or walker | 4-point contact → base of support > COM projection |
| Single below-knee amputation (healed) | Axillary or forearm crutch (temporary), then prosthesis | Energy cost 0.30 ml/kg·m (crutch) vs 0.20 (prosthesis) |
| Permanent bilateral amputation / paraplegia | Manual wheelchair (short term), powered (long term) | ECW wheelchair ≈ 0.10 ml/kg·m, lower than double-crutch |
| Upper-limb weakness + lower-limb impairment | Powered wheelchair with chin/head control | Removes upper-limb actuation requirement |
| Elderly with mild balance deficit | Hemi-walker / pick-up walker | Wide base, lightweight, single-hand use |
| Cognitive impairment + mobility issue | Rollator with seat & brakes | Auto-lock brakes prevent runaway on slope |

---

### 1.3.5 Step-by-Step Analysis — Static Stability of a Quad Cane

A quad cane has four feet in a rectangular base of $a \times b$ where typically $a = 18$ cm (front-back) and $b = 30$ cm (side-side). The user stands centred on the cane.

**Step 1 — Restoring torque when cane tips laterally**

When the cane is on the verge of tipping sideways, the line of action of the user's load (passed through the centre of the cane shaft, $h$ above the floor) must fall **inside** the support base.

**Step 2 — Critical tip angle (about the side edge)**

$$
\tan \theta_{critical} = \frac{b/2}{h}
$$

For a typical cane with $h = 90$ cm and $b = 30$ cm:

$$
\tan \theta_{critical} = \frac{0.15}{0.90} = 0.1667
$$

$$
\theta_{critical} = \arctan(0.1667) = 9.46^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Safety margin**

A KTU-accepted design rule is **safety factor ≥ 1.5**:

$$
\theta_{design} = \frac{\theta_{critical}}{1.5} = 6.31^\circ
$$

> A quad cane is stable up to ~9.5° lateral tilt, which is **vastly superior** to a single-point cane (~3.5°) — this is the engineering reason for the prescription upgrade.

---

### 1.3.6 Numerical — Step Length Recovery with Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO)

**Problem setup:** A patient with foot-drop walks without aid (step length = 0.45 m, cadence 70 steps/min). With an AFO, step length becomes 0.60 m and cadence 80 steps/min. Compute the speed index.

**Step 1 — Compute speed before and after**

$$
v_{before} = 0.45 \times 70 = 31.5 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
v_{after} = 0.60 \times 80 = 48.0 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Speed index**

$$
\text{Speed Index} = \frac{48.0}{31.5} = 1.524
$$

> AFO delivers a **52.4 % improvement** in walking speed for foot-drop — a strong, quantifiable justification for the orthosis prescription.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — ICF-Based Functional Classification of Assistive Devices (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Person with Disability]
    H0 --> H1[Body Function Impairment]
    H0 --> H2[Activity Limitation]
    H0 --> H3[Participation Restriction]

    H1 --> A1[Mobility Assistance]
    H1 --> A2[Self-Care ADL Assistance]
    H2 --> A3[Sensory Assistance]
    H2 --> A4[Communication Assistance]
    H3 --> A5[Cognitive Assistance]

    A1 --> B1[Canes Crutches Walkers]
    A1 --> B2[Manual Wheelchairs]
    A1 --> B3[Powered Wheelchairs]
    A1 --> B4[Standing Transfer Aids]

    A2 --> C1[Bathing Toileting Aids]
    A2 --> C2[Dressing Aids]
    A2 --> C3[Eating Aids]

    A3 --> D1[Visual Aids White Cane Braille]
    A3 --> D2[Hearing Aids Cochlear Implants]

    A4 --> E1[Low Tech AAC Picture Boards]
    A4 --> E2[High Tech AAC Eye Gaze]

    A5 --> F1[Memory Aids Pill Reminders]
    A5 --> F2[Executive Aids GPS Wayfinders]
```

### Diagram 2 — HAAT Model Block Diagram for Assistive Device Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Human Domain]
        HU[Residual Strength]
        HV[Range of Motion]
        HC[Cognition]
        HS[Sensation]
    end
    subgraph S2[Activity Domain]
        AT1[Walk]
        AT2[Eat]
        AT3[Communicate]
        AT4[Read]
    end
    subgraph S3[Assistive Technology Domain]
        DV1[Wheelchair]
        DV2[Built Up Cutlery]
        DV3[AAC Device]
        DV4[Screen Reader]
    end
    subgraph S4[Context Domain]
        CX1[Home]
        CX2[Workplace]
        CX3[Outdoor Public]
    end

    HU --> AT1
    HV --> AT2
    HC --> AT3
    HS --> AT4
    AT1 --> DV1
    AT2 --> DV2
    AT3 --> DV3
    AT4 --> DV4
    DV1 --> CX1
    DV2 --> CX2
    DV3 --> CX3
    CX1 --> HU
    CX2 --> HU
    CX3 --> HU
```

### Diagram 3 — Mobility-Aid Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Patient able to walk at all?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need upper limbs to push?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M1[Manual Wheelchair]
    Q2 -- No --> M2[Powered Wheelchair]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Severe balance loss?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> M3[Walker or Rollator]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Unilateral or Bilateral weakness?}
    Q4 -- Unilateral --> M4[Single Point or Quad Cane]
    Q4 -- Bilateral --> M5[Forearm Crutches or Axillary Crutches]
    M1 --> O1[Add Cushion Postural Support]
    M2 --> O2[Add Joystick Chin Control]
    M3 --> O3[Add Brakes Seat]
    M4 --> O4[Set Handle at Wrist Crease]
    M5 --> O5[Adjust 2 to 3 Finger Widths Below Axilla]
```

### Diagram 4 — Block Architecture of a Powered Wheelchair System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Battery Pack 24V Lead Acid or Li-ion] --> P2[Motor Controller PWM Driver]
    P2 --> P3[DC Motors with Gearbox]
    P3 --> P4[Drive Wheels]
    P5[Joystick or Head Array Input] --> P6[Microcontroller Unit]
    P6 --> P2
    P7[Tilt Sensors] --> P6
    P8[Brake System Electromagnetic] --> P3
    P9[Battery Level Indicator Buzzer] --> P1
```

> [!TIP]
> When Mermaid cannot render a physical sketch (e.g., anatomical AFO), use the **block-functional architecture** above — examiners value **system-level thinking** over hand-drawn copies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEEBT834]`
**Define Assistive Technology. List any four categories of assistive devices with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1 + 2):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** "Any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially, modified or customised, used to increase, maintain or improve functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities." (US PL 100-407 / WHO).
- **Four categories (½ mark each, total 2 marks):**
  1. **Mobility assistance** — e.g., manual wheelchair.
  2. **Sensory assistance** — e.g., white cane for the visually impaired.
  3. **Communication assistance (AAC)** — e.g., picture-exchange board.
  4. **Self-care / ADL assistance** — e.g., long-handled reacher.

---

#### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEEBT834]`
**Distinguish between orthosis and prosthesis. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Orthosis (1½ marks):** An *external device applied to a body segment* to **support, align, prevent, or correct deformity** or to **improve function** of the existing limb. *Example:* Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO) for foot drop.
- **Prosthesis (1½ marks):** A *substitute device that replaces an absent body part*, typically a missing limb, restoring as much function and form as possible. *Example:* Trans-tibial (below-knee) prosthesis with SACH foot.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, OEEBT834]`

**Q. (a)** Classify **mobility assistive devices** with a clear functional taxonomy. With the help of neat diagrams, explain the engineering working principle and biomechanical justification of:
**(i) Axillary crutch** (5 marks)
**(ii) Manual wheelchair with hand-rim propulsion** (2 marks)

**Q. (b)** A 75 kg patient is being prescribed a **wheelchair** to climb a 1:15 ramp. Compute the **sustainable push force per hand-rim** and **comment on the design choice** between manual and powered wheelchair. Use $\mu_r = 0.018$. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Classification (4 marks) + Crutch (5 marks) + Wheelchair (2 marks):**

1. **Classification tree (4 marks):**
   - **Mobility aids** divide into:
     - **Ambulation aids (user retains upright walking):**
       - Single-point canes (straight cane, offset cane)
       - Quad canes (small-base, large-base)
       - Crutches (axillary, forearm/Lofstrand, platform/gutter)
       - Walkers (pick-up, rollator, hemi-walker, posterior)
     - **Wheeled mobility aids (user seated):**
       - Manual wheelchairs (self-propelled, attendant-propelled, transit)
       - Powered wheelchairs (rear-wheel, mid-wheel, front-wheel drive)
       - Powered scooters (3- or 4-wheeled)
     - **Transfer & standing aids:** stand-up wheelchairs, patient lifters.

2. **Axillary crutch — engineering principle (5 marks):**
   - The axillary crutch is a **two-bar reverse-lever** with the user's elbow as the fulcrum.
   - Load path: ground → rubber tip → lower bar → handgrip → upper bar → axillary pad → ribcage of the user.
   - **Biomechanical justification:**
     - The crutch transfers ~25–50 % of body weight from the lower limb to the **upper limb and chest wall**, sparing the injured leg.
     - Axillary pad is **not a true weight-bearing surface** (risk of axillary nerve palsy if misused); the handgrip bears the bulk of the load.
     - **Adjustment rule:** handgrip set at **wrist crease** (elbow flexed 15–30°), top pad **2–3 finger-widths below axilla**.
   - Energy cost on axillary crutches ≈ 0.60–0.70 ml O₂/kg·m, which is 3–4× normal walking.

3. **Manual wheelchair (2 marks):**
   - Two large rear wheels with **hand-rims** (smaller coaxial discs) and two small front castors.
   - **Hand-rim propulsion = class-2 lever:** user applies tangential force on rim; force is transmitted to tyre via friction with the floor.
   - Mechanical advantage = (hand-rim radius) / (tyre radius) ≈ 1.0–1.5.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 75 + 15 = 90$ kg (patient + chair), $g = 9.81$ m/s², slope 1:15, $\mu_r = 0.018$.

**Step 1 — Ramp angle**

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{1}{15} \Rightarrow \alpha = \arctan(0.0667) = 3.814^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Sine & Cosine**

$$
\sin \alpha = 0.0665, \quad \cos \alpha = 0.9978
$$

**Step 3 — Total weight**

$$
W = 90 \times 9.81 = 882.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Total push force**

$$
F_{total} = W \sin \alpha + \mu_r W \cos \alpha
$$

$$
F_{total} = 882.9 \times 0.0665 + 0.018 \times 882.9 \times 0.9978
$$

$$
F_{total} = 58.71 + 15.86 = 74.57 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Per-rim force**

$$
F_{rim} = \frac{74.57}{2} = 37.28 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on design choice (2 marks)**

- 37.28 N per rim is **within sustainable ergonomic range** (30–60 N) for a healthy adult.
- However, the patient is **already 75 kg and using a chair**, indicating possible **low cardiopulmonary reserve**; combined with age-related weakness, a **powered wheelchair is the safer prescription** for regular ramp use.
- Manual wheelchair remains acceptable for occasional, short ramps; a **rear-wheel-drive powered chair with anti-tip wheels** is recommended for daily outdoor use.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Mark-Loss Callout (Part B 14-mark):**
> 1. Forgetting to **add the wheelchair mass to the user's mass** (–2 marks).
> 2. Not converting the **slope ratio 1:15 to an angle** correctly (–1 mark).
> 3. Failing to **distribute the total force across two rims** (–2 marks).
> 4. Skipping the **design-comment step** at the end — examiners mark 2 marks specifically for engineering judgement, not just arithmetic.

---

#### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative, OEEBT834]`

**Q. (a)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the **HAAT model** for assistive technology selection. Apply it to a **60-year-old post-stroke patient with right hemiparesis** who wishes to return to office work. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Derive the **critical tip-angle of a single-point cane** with handle height 90 cm and tip-base width 4 cm. Discuss how a **quad cane improves the lateral stability** and quantify the improvement. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — HAAT Model (7 marks):**

1. **HAAT model block diagram (3 marks):** The model (Cook & Hussey) has four interacting blocks — **Human → Activity → Assistive Technology → Context** — with feedback loops. *(Draw the same flowchart as in SECTION_4 Diagram 2.)*

2. **Application to the case (4 marks):**

| Domain | Patient Profile | Engineering Match |
|---|---|---|
| **Human** | Right hemiparesis: weak grip, spastic gait, intact cognition | Residual strengths: left upper limb, vision, hearing |
| **Activity** | Office work — typing, writing, mobility in office, using washroom | Sub-tasks: ambulate in corridor, transfer to chair, use keyboard, use toilet |
| **AT** | **Offset cane (left hand)**, **built-up keyboard with keyguard**, **left-hand mouse (trackball)**, **grab-bar in toilet** | Each device matches a residual ability to an activity demand |
| **Context** | Office with lift, accessible washroom, ergonomic chair | Wheelchair-accessible desk; software with voice input as backup |

- **Justification (1 mark):** The HAAT framework ensures **no single device is prescribed in isolation** — the cane supports ambulation, the trackball bypasses right-hand weakness, and the office context (lift + accessible toilet) enables participation.

**Part (b) — Critical Tip-Angle Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define critical tip-angle**

The single-point cane behaves as an **inverted pendulum** pivoting on its tip. The line of action of the user's effective weight (passing through the cane-grip axis) must remain within the base of support.

**Step 2 — Geometry**

For a single-point cane, the **effective tip-base width** ≈ tip diameter, modelled as $b = 4$ cm (rubber ferrule diameter). The cane acts as a rigid stick of length $h = 90$ cm.

**Step 3 — Critical tip-angle equation**

$$
\tan \theta_{c} = \frac{b/2}{h} = \frac{0.02}{0.90} = 0.02222
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**

$$
\theta_{c} = \arctan(0.02222) = 1.273^\circ
$$

**Step 5 — Quad cane improvement**

A large-base quad cane has $b \approx 30$ cm.

$$
\tan \theta_{c,quad} = \frac{0.15}{0.90} = 0.1667 \Rightarrow \theta_{c,quad} = 9.46^\circ
$$

**Step 6 — Quantify improvement**

$$
\text{Improvement factor} = \frac{\theta_{c,quad}}{\theta_{c,single}} = \frac{9.46^\circ}{1.27^\circ} \approx 7.45
$$

> The quad cane offers a **7.45× improvement** in lateral tip-angle, at the cost of bulk and stair-climb difficulty. For a post-stroke patient with significant balance loss, this is a **clinically meaningful trade-off** justifying the prescription.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b) 14-mark Alternative:**
> 1. Confusing the cane length $h$ with handle height (use 90 cm, do not substitute user's height).
> 2. Forgetting to **convert cm to m** before taking the ratio (causes silent error).
> 3. Not stating the **safety factor** or **typical design margin** — examiners award 1 mark for that.
> 4. Skipping the **quad-vs-single trade-off discussion** at the end.

---

### 1.5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition (one-liner):** Assistive Technology is any device that increases, maintains, or improves functional capability of a person with disability.
- [ ] **Five AT categories:** Mobility, ADL/Self-care, Sensory, Communication, Cognitive.
- [ ] **Three mobility-aid sub-classes:** Ambulation aids (canes, crutches, walkers), Wheeled mobility (manual, powered, scooter), Transfer aids.
- [ ] **Crutch adjustment rule:** Top pad **2–3 finger-widths below axilla**; handgrip at **wrist crease**, elbow flexed **15–30°**.
- [ ] **Cane adjustment rule:** Handle at **greater trochanter / wrist crease** with arm hanging loose.
- [ ] **ECW normal:** **0.15–0.20 ml O₂/kg·m**; on axillary crutches it rises to **0.60–0.70**; on unilateral prosthesis **0.30**.
- [ ] **Wheelchair push force sustainable range:** **30–60 N per hand-rim**.
- [ ] **Hand-rim MA = rim radius / tyre radius** (typically 1.0–1.5).
- [ ] **ADA / RPwD 2016 ramp limit:** **1:12 slope** (≈ 4.76°).
- [ ] **Quad cane tip-angle ≈ 9.5°**, single-point cane ≈ 1.3° — quad is **~7× more stable laterally**.
- [ ] **Static load limits (must know):** Cane **113 kg**, axillary crutch **160 kg**, standard manual wheelchair **113 kg**, bariatric **180+ kg**.
- [ ] **Contact pressure under cane tip:** ≈ 0.35 MPa for 70 kg user (within vinyl/wood limits).
- [ ] **HAAT model blocks:** Human → Activity → Assistive Technology → Context (with feedback).
- [ ] **WHO-ICF trio:** Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction (engineer intervenes at the second two).
- [ ] **AFO benefit:** Speed index **> 1.5** for foot-drop (must remember the 52 % figure).
- [ ] **Key Indian standards:** BIS, RPwD Act 2016, Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules 2017, UN CRPD 2006.
- [ ] **Key international standard for AT classification:** **ISO 9999:2016** — Assistive products for persons with disability — Classification and terminology.
- [ ] **Forbidden phrases in KTU answers:** "aids and appliances" (old terminology), "handicapped" (use "person with disability"), "suffering from" (use "person with").
- [ ] **Always end a 14-mark answer with an engineering comment** — never leave the design implications blank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Mantra (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Quantify everything you can. "Improves mobility" gets 0 marks; "reduces ECW from 0.65 to 0.30 ml O₂/kg·m, a 54 % reduction" gets full marks.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rehabilitation Technology** is the systematic application of scientific, engineering, biomedical, and clinical knowledge to design, develop, test, evaluate, and apply technological solutions that assist individuals with disabilities in achieving their maximum functional independence, social participation, and quality of life.

According to the **AAATE (Association for the Advancement of Assistive Technology in Europe)**, **Assistive Technology (AT)** is any device, equipment, system, or service — including their acquisition, design, manufacturing, fitting, and ongoing support — that helps a person with a disability to perform a function or activity that would otherwise be difficult or impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The terms *Rehabilitation Technology* and *Assistive Technology* are often used interchangeably, but rehabilitation technology is the broader umbrella that includes the *engineering process* — from problem identification, design, prototyping, clinical testing, to technology transfer — whereas assistive technology refers to the *end product* or service used by the consumer.

The **World Health Organization (WHO)** further classifies technology-based interventions into four levels:

1. **Medical Technology** — for prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of disease (e.g., MRI, ventilators).
2. **Assistive Technology** — for functional improvement in daily living.
3. **Rehabilitation Technology** — broader category encompassing engineering of assistive devices.
4. **Inclusive/Universal Technology** — mainstream products designed for all (e.g., curb cuts, voice assistants).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Eyeglass Story:**
> Imagine a person who cannot read the blackboard clearly because of refractive error. A simple pair of spectacles (lenses mounted in a frame) instantly converts the visually impaired task into a manageable one. The **lens** is engineered using optical physics, the **frame** is ergonomic, and the **prescription** is determined through clinical testing. This small device — invented around the 13th century — is arguably the world's first widely-deployed assistive technology.
>
> Now scale this idea: a powered wheelchair, a cochlear implant, or a screen reader is the same concept — *engineering applied to compensate a functional limitation*. Rehabilitation engineering is simply the *organized, scientific, and ethical process* of doing this at scale, across hearing, vision, mobility, cognition, and communication domains.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** — WHO framework: `2001`
- **WHO Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology (GATE)** initiative: launched `2014`
- **AAATE** formal definition adopted: `2003`
- **RESNA** (Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America) terminology standards
- **ISO 9999:2022** — International standard for classification of assistive products, defining **12 classes** and **~1300 product codes**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Technology" is a foundational topic in Module 1:**
> Before a student studies prosthetic knees, FES systems, or augmentative communication, they must first understand *what qualifies as rehabilitation technology*, *how it is classified*, *who delivers it*, and *how it moves from lab to the user*. This module lays that conceptual foundation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content Sheet

### 2.1 The Scope of Rehabilitation Technology — Structured Logic

Rehabilitation technology operates across a wide spectrum of human function. The structured logic of why it exists is as follows:

- **Impairment** (body structure/function loss) →
- **Activity Limitation** (difficulty in task execution) →
- **Participation Restriction** (inability to engage in life situations) →
- **Contextual Factors** (environmental and personal barriers) →
- **Technology Intervention** to mitigate the chain above

The engineering response is therefore classified by the **functional domain** it addresses:

1. **Mobility & Postural Support** — wheelchairs, walkers, standing frames, prosthetics, orthotics
2. **Sensory Augmentation** — hearing aids, cochlear implants, low-vision devices, tactile displays
3. **Communication & Cognition** — AAC devices, memory aids, alerting systems
4. **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** — adapted utensils, environmental control units (ECUs)
5. **Seating & Positioning** — custom-molded cushions, seating systems
6. **Vocational & Educational** — computer access, screen readers, adaptive switches

### 2.2 Models of Disability — A Critical Foundation

The philosophy of "technology" depends on the *model* of disability adopted:

| Model | Core Idea | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Model** | Disability resides in the individual's body; "fix" the person | Focus on assistive devices, prosthetics, surgical correction |
| **Social Model** | Disability is created by societal barriers | Focus on universal design, environmental modification, accessible infrastructure |
| **Biopsychosocial Model (ICF)** | Interaction of body, activity, participation, and context | Holistic design — device + environment + policy + personal factors |
| **Functional Model** | Focus on what the person can/cannot do in context | Task-oriented, activity-based technology design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **ICF model (WHO, 2001)** replaced the older **ICIDH (1980)** classification. A 14-mark question on "models of disability" is extremely high-probability. Always mention the shift from ICIDH → ICF and the *biopsychosocial* perspective.

### 2.3 The ICF Framework — Structural Analysis

The ICF classifies functioning and disability through two main parts, each with components:

$$\text{ICF} = \{ \text{Body Functions \& Structure},\ \text{Activities \& Participation} \} \times \{ \text{Environmental Factors},\ \text{Personal Factors} \}$$

- **Body Functions (b)** — physiological/psychological functions
- **Body Structures (s)** — anatomical parts
- **Activities and Participation (d)** — execution of tasks and involvement in life situations
- **Environmental Factors (e)** — physical, social, attitudinal environment
- **Personal Factors** — age, gender, motivation, lifestyle (not classified in ICF but acknowledged)

Each component is qualified by a qualifier — typically a scale from `0` (no problem) to `4` (complete problem):

$$Q_{d} = \frac{\text{Extent of participation restriction}}{\text{Universal expectation without condition}}$$

### 2.4 Classification of Assistive Technology — ISO 9999:2022

The **ISO 9999:2022** standard divides assistive products into **12 main classes**, each with hierarchical sub-classes. The key classes are:

| Class Code | Class Name | Example Products |
|---|---|---|
| 04 | Aids for personal medical treatment | Injection devices, dialysis support |
| 05 | Aids for training in skills | Braille trainers, mobility trainers |
| 06 | Orthoses and prostheses | Artificial limbs, spinal braces |
| 09 | Aids for personal care and protection | Incontinence products, protective eyewear |
| 12 | Aids for personal mobility | Wheelchairs, walking frames, crutches |
| 15 | Aids for housekeeping | Adapted kitchen tools, robotic vacuum |
| 18 | Furnishings and adaptations to homes | Ramps, grab rails, height-adjustable beds |
| 21 | Aids for communication, information, signaling | AAC devices, screen readers, hearing aids |
| 22 | Aids for handling objects and devices | Adapted handles, robotic manipulators |
| 24 | Aids for controlling, carrying, moving objects | Environmental control units (ECU) |
| 27 | Aids for recreation | Adapted sports equipment, assistive gaming |
| 30 | Aids for employment and vocational training | Sit-stand workstations, ergonomic chairs |

### 2.5 The Service Delivery Model — Step-by-Step Logic

Rehabilitation technology is not merely a product; it is a **service**. The structured delivery model is:

1. **Referral & Intake** — client enters the system (hospital, school, NGO)
2. **Assessment** — multidisciplinary evaluation of client needs
3. **Goal Setting** — SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)
4. **Device Selection / Custom Design** — matching product to user
5. **Fitting & Training** — user learns to operate the device
6. **Follow-up & Maintenance** — periodic review, repair, replacement
7. **Outcome Measurement** — did the device improve function/participation?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** This model is the *standard workflow* used in rehabilitation centers worldwide, including institutions like NIEPMD (Chennai), NILD (Kolkata), and composite regional centers (CRCs) under the **Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities (DEPwD), Government of India**.

### 2.6 The Multidisciplinary Team (MDT)

Rehabilitation technology is delivered by an **interdisciplinary team**:

- **Rehabilitation Engineer** (B.Tech/M.Tech in Biomedical/ Rehabilitation Engineering) — design, fabrication, customization
- **Physiatrist** (Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation doctor) — clinical lead
- **Physical Therapist (PT)** — gait, mobility, strength
- **Occupational Therapist (OT)** — ADL, upper-limb function
- **Prosthetist & Orthotist (P&O)** — limb and bracing devices
- **Speech-Language Pathologist (SLP)** — communication, swallowing
- **Clinical Psychologist** — cognitive and emotional aspects
- **Social Worker** — community reintegration, funding
- **Special Educator** — learning and vocational needs
- **User (Person with Disability) and Family** — central decision-makers

### 2.7 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Expression / Definition | Unit / Scale | Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| ICF Qualifier Scale | $Q \in \left\{ 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4 \right\}$ | `0 = None, 4 = Complete` | 3-mark definitional |
| Disability Prevalence (WHO) | $\sim 15\%$ of world population | Global estimate | 3-mark fact |
| ISO 9999 Classes | $N_{classes} = 12$ | Standard | 3-mark fact |
| Technology Transfer Stages | $N_{stages} = 6$ | Concept→Lab→Prototype→Field→Market→Policy | 7-mark descriptive |
| Service Delivery Steps | $N_{steps} = 7$ | Intake→Follow-up | 7-mark descriptive |
| AAATE Definition Year | $Y_{AAATE} = 2003$ | Year | 3-mark |
| ICF Adoption Year | $Y_{ICF} = 2001$ | Year | 3-mark |
| GATE Initiative | $Y_{GATE} = 2014$ | Year | 3-mark |

### 2.8 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Fields

Rehabilitation technology is a **convergence field** drawing from:
- **Mechanical Engineering** — prosthetics, wheelchairs, robotics
- **Electronics & Electrical** — ECUs, hearing aids, neural stimulators
- **Computer Science** — AAC software, AI-based sign language recognition
- **Biomedical Engineering** — biosensors, brain-computer interfaces
- **Materials Science** — smart materials, biocompatible polymers
- **Industrial Design** — ergonomics, universal design principles

This interdisciplinary nature makes it one of the most **employment-rich, socially impactful, and ethically grounded** branches of engineering — directly aligned with the **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 3, 10, 11)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Technology Transfer & Service Delivery

### 3.1 The Six-Stage Technology Transfer Process

Rehabilitation technology does not appear in the market spontaneously. It follows a structured **technology transfer pipeline** that engineers must understand. The derivation-style breakdown:

**Step 1 — Needs Identification (Conceptualization)**
The process begins by identifying a real unmet need in the disabled community. Methods include surveys, focus groups, ethnographic observation in homes, and clinic-based needs assessments.

$$N_{identified} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Need}_i \quad \text{where } k = \text{number of distinct user problems}$$

**Step 2 — Basic Research & Concept Development**
Engineers and researchers develop the underlying scientific basis — sensors, actuators, materials, algorithms. Literature review, patent search, and proof-of-concept experiments dominate this stage.

$$C_{concept} = f(\text{Physics},\ \text{Biology},\ \text{Materials},\ \text{Controls})$$

**Step 3 — Prototype Design & Laboratory Testing**
A working prototype is built and tested in lab conditions against performance specifications such as:
- Durability (cycles of use)
- Safety (fail-safe modes)
- Ergonomics (fit to anthropometric ranges)
- Cost (manufacturability)

The verification follows:

$$P_{prototype}(t) = P_{specified} \pm \epsilon \quad \text{where } \epsilon = \text{tolerance band}$$

**Step 4 — Clinical & Field Trials**
Prototypes are tested on actual users under real-world conditions. **IRB (Institutional Review Board)** and **IEC (Informed Ethical Consent)** approvals are mandatory. Outcome measures include:
- Functional Independence Measure (FIM)
- Barthel Index
- Quality-of-life scales (WHOQOL-DIS)
- Goal Attainment Scaling (GAS)

**Step 5 — Commercialization & Market Distribution**
The product is scaled for manufacturing. Regulatory approvals (**CDSCO** in India, **FDA** in the USA, **CE** in Europe) are required. Pricing, distribution, and after-sales service networks are established.

**Step 6 — Policy Integration & Universal Access**
Successful technologies feed into national schemes. In India, examples include:
- **ADIP (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances)** scheme
- **RPwD Act 2016** (Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act)
- **Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan** (Accessible India Campaign)

### 3.2 Quantitative Reasoning — Matching Technology to Need

Consider a user with **C5 spinal cord injury** (tetraplegia, limited arm/hand function). The engineer must solve:

**Given:**
- User can flex elbow (biceps intact), cannot grasp objects effectively
- User has intact cognition and vision
- Goal: enable independent computer use for education/employment

**Engineering Decision Pathway:**

$$\text{Need} \rightarrow \text{Function} \rightarrow \text{Interface} \rightarrow \text{Device}$$

Step 1: Need = Computer access
Step 2: Function required = Switch activation
Step 3: Interface options = Sip-and-puff, chin joystick, head tracking, blink detection, voice control
Step 4: Device selection = Head-tracking mouse + on-screen keyboard + speech-to-text

The matching is governed by:

$$M_{score} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_j$$

where $w_j$ is the importance weight of criterion $j$ (e.g., reliability, cost, ease of use) and $s_j$ is the score (0–10) of the device on that criterion.

### 3.3 The Cost–Functionality Trade-off — Derivation of Affordability Equation

A key engineering challenge in assistive technology is balancing cost with function. A simplified model:

$$C_{total} = C_{device} + C_{training} + C_{maintenance} + C_{replacement} \cdot T_{life}^{-1}$$

where:
- $C_{device}$ = purchase cost
- $C_{training}$ = user training cost
- $C_{maintenance}$ = annual upkeep
- $C_{replacement}$ = replacement device cost
- $T_{life}$ = device life in years

**Example calculation (a basic manual wheelchair in India):**

- $C_{device} = \text{₹} 8{,}000$
- $C_{training} = \text{₹} 1{,}000$
- $C_{maintenance} = \text{₹} 500 / \text{year}$
- $C_{replacement} = \text{₹} 8{,}000$
- $T_{life} = 5\ \text{years}$

$$C_{total} = 8000 + 1000 + 500 \times 5 + 8000 \times 5^{-1}$$

$$C_{total} = 8000 + 1000 + 2500 + 1600 = \text{₹} 13{,}100$$

This **₹ 13,100 over 5 years** is the *real cost of independence* — a critical value used in justifying ADIP scheme funding.

### 3.4 Outcome Measurement — Derivation of Functional Gain

A common clinical outcome measure is the **FIM (Functional Independence Measure)** score, ranging from 18 (total dependence) to 126 (total independence).

$$\Delta_{FIM} = FIM_{post} - FIM_{pre}$$

$$\text{Effectiveness} = \frac{\Delta_{FIM}}{FIM_{max} - FIM_{pre}} \times 100\%$$

For a person with a stroke using a reacher tool:
- $FIM_{pre} = 60$ (moderate dependence)
- $FIM_{post} = 80$ (modified independence)
- $FIM_{max} = 126$

$$\Delta_{FIM} = 80 - 60 = 20$$

$$\text{Effectiveness} = \frac{20}{126 - 60} \times 100\% = \frac{20}{66} \times 100\% \approx 30.3\%$$

This **~30% functional gain** quantifies the technology's value — useful in research publications and in justifying insurance/tender purchases.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
