# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define GPS. List the three segments of GPS and state the function of the Control Segment.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based navigation system that provides three-dimensional position, velocity, and time (PVT) information to a user with a compatible receiver, anywhere on or near Earth, by measuring the time-of-flight of signals from a constellation of Medium Earth Orbit (MEO) satellites.

The **three segments** are:
1. **Space Segment:** Constellation of ≥ 24 satellites in 6 orbital planes at 20,200 km altitude.
2. **Control Segment:** Master Control Station, Monitor Stations, and Ground Antennas. **Function:** Tracks satellites, computes orbital corrections, and uploads navigation messages to maintain system accuracy and integrity.
3. **User Segment:** GPS receivers (handheld, vehicle, wearable) that passively listen to satellite signals and compute user position.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is Dilution of Precision (DOP)? Mention the types and state why a low PDOP is desirable.**

**Model Answer:**

> **DOP** is a dimensionless geometric factor that quantifies how the **3D arrangement of visible satellites** affects the positional accuracy of a GPS fix. It is derived from the inverse of the design matrix $H$ as $\text{DOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1})}$.

**Types:** GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP.

A **low PDOP (≤ 4)** indicates that satellites are well-distributed across the sky, producing well-conditioned geometry — even small measurement errors yield small position errors. A **high PDOP (≥ 6)** indicates clustered satellites, which amplifies range errors into large position errors.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of GPS with a block diagram of the receiver architecture. Derive the pseudorange equation and explain why **four satellites** are required for a 3D fix.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the major sources of error in GPS positioning and explain how DGPS improves accuracy. State the role of A-GPS in wearable rehabilitation devices.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Working Principle of GPS:**

GPS uses **one-way time-of-flight (ToF) ranging** combined with **trilateration**.

1. **Each satellite** continuously transmits a unique **C/A (Coarse Acquisition) code** modulated on the **L1 carrier (1575.42 MHz)**. The transmission time is precisely tagged using the satellite's **atomic clock**.
2. **The receiver** generates a replica of the same C/A code and shifts it in time until correlation peaks with the incoming signal. The required time shift equals the signal travel time.
3. **Pseudorange** $\rho_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i$ is computed. Because the receiver's quartz clock is not synchronized with the satellite's atomic clock, the range is "pseudo."
4. The receiver solves a system of four such pseudorange equations (4 satellites) for the four unknowns: $x_u, y_u, z_u, b$.

**Why Four Satellites?**

Three equations from three satellites would be sufficient in principle to solve for $x_u, y_u, z_u$ if both clocks were perfectly synchronized. In practice, the receiver clock has an unknown bias $b$ (in meters). Including $b$ as a fourth unknown **requires a fourth satellite**, providing one extra equation and over-determining the system only after $b$ is also solved.

**Derivation of the Pseudorange Equation:**

Let $t_s^{(i)}$ be the transmission time tagged by satellite $i$'s clock and $t_u$ the reception time tagged by the receiver clock. The true geometric range is:

$$R_i = c \cdot (t_{arrive} - t_s^{(i)})$$

where $t_{arrive}$ is the true arrival time. Since the receiver's clock has bias $\delta t_u$:

$$t_u = t_{arrive} + \delta t_u$$

The **measured** (pseudo) range becomes:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_u - t_s^{(i)}) = c \cdot (t_{arrive} - t_s^{(i)}) + c \cdot \delta t_u$$

Defining $b = c \cdot \delta t_u$:

$$\boxed{\rho_i = R_i + b}$$

This is the **fundamental pseudorange equation**. The four unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, b)$ require four independent $\rho_i$ values, hence **four satellites**.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Major Sources of Error in GPS:**

| Error Source | Magnitude (m, 1σ) | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Ionospheric delay | 4.0 | Dual-frequency, Klobuchar model |
| Ephemeris error | 2.1 | SBAS, updated almanac |
| Satellite clock error | 2.1 | Uploaded clock corrections |
| Tropospheric delay | 0.7 | Saastamoinen model, refractivity index |
| Multipath | 1.4 | Choke-ring antennas, RAIM |
| Receiver noise | 0.5 | High-quality RF front-end |

**Differential GPS (DGPS):**

DGPS uses a **reference receiver at a precisely known location** to compute **range corrections** $\Delta \rho$ and broadcasts them to nearby roving receivers. The rover applies:

$$\rho_{corrected} = \rho_{measured} + \Delta \rho$$

This cancels **spatially correlated errors** (ionosphere, troposphere, satellite clock), improving accuracy from ~5–10 m to **1–3 m**, or even **sub-meter** with RTK.

**A-GPS (Assisted GPS) in Rehabilitation Wearables:**

A-GPS uses an **assistance server** (cellular network) to:
1. Provide the approximate orbital data (almanac) so the receiver skips satellite search.
2. Provide a coarse time reference from the cellular network.
3. Offload position computation to the server.

**Benefit for rehabilitation wearables:**
- **TTFF reduced** from 30–60 s (cold start) to **< 5 s (warm start)**.
- **Lower power** consumption — critical for battery-limited wristbands and pendants.
- **Better indoor sensitivity** — partial signal lock possible, useful for indoor-outdoor transitions in dementia care facilities.

---

### ⭐ Question B [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the GPS signal structure. Explain the difference between C/A code and P(Y) code. What is the navigation message?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain a wearable GPS tracking system for dementia patients. Discuss its ethical considerations (privacy, consent, autonomy).

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**GPS Signal Structure (Layered Modulation):**

Each GPS satellite transmits on three carriers: **L1, L2, and L5**. Each carrier is modulated by one or more codes:

- **L1 (1575.42 MHz)**: Modulated by C/A code, P(Y) code, and Navigation message.
- **L2 (1227.60 MHz)**: Modulated by P(Y) code and Navigation message.
- **L5 (1176.45 MHz)**: Modulated by two codes (I5 and Q5) and Navigation message.

**C/A Code (Coarse Acquisition Code):**

- 1023-chip **Gold code** at chip rate **1.023 Mcps (mega-chips per second)**.
- Repeats every **1 millisecond**.
- Unique to each satellite (PRN).
- Used by **civilian** SPS (Standard Positioning Service).
- Provides ~3 m range resolution (chip length = 293 m).

**P(Y) Code (Precise Code):**

- Chip rate **10.23 Mcps** — ten times faster.
- Length: **2.35 × 10¹⁴ chips**, repeating once per week.
- Anti-spoofing: encrypted to Y-code for authorized military users (PPS).
- Provides ~30 cm range resolution.

**Navigation Message:**

A 50 bps data stream modulated onto the carriers. Contains:
1. **Ephemeris data** — precise orbital parameters of the transmitting satellite.
2. **Almanac data** — coarse orbital info for all satellites (used for satellite acquisition).
3. **Clock correction parameters** — to model satellite atomic clock drift.
4. **Ionospheric model coefficients** (Klobuchar model) for L1 single-frequency users.
5. **Health and status flags.**

**Time to transmit the full message:** **12.5 minutes** (25 frames × 30 s/frame).

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Wearable GPS Tracking System for Dementia Patients — Block Diagram:**

*(See SECTION 4.4 — Wearable GPS Wristband Functional Block Architecture.)*

**System Components:**

1. **GPS Module** — receives satellite signals, outputs NMEA 0183 sentences (lat/long) at 1 Hz.
2. **Microcontroller** — parses GPS data, runs geofence algorithm, detects wandering.
3. **GSM/LTE Modem** — transmits location to caregiver's cloud platform via SMS or TCP/IP.
4. **Accelerometer / Fall Sensor** — detects falls and triggers SOS.
5. **Battery** — typically 600–1000 mAh Li-ion, 48–72 hour standby.
6. **Caregiver Interface** — smartphone app with map view, geofence editor, alert log.

**Geofencing Algorithm:**

The patient's safe zone is defined as a polygon (circle, square, or arbitrary). When GPS position falls outside this zone for a configurable dwell-time (e.g., 60 s), the system triggers an SMS alert:

```text
[Caregiver Alert]
Patient: Mr. K
Lat: 10.0261 N
Lng: 76.3125 E
Time: 14:32 IST
Outside Safe Zone
```

**Ethical Considerations:**

1. **Privacy vs. Safety Trade-off:** Continuous location tracking can be perceived as surveillance, eroding patient dignity.
2. **Informed Consent:** Patients with cognitive decline may not legally consent; **surrogate consent** from legal guardian is required, but the patient's residual assent should be sought.
3. **Autonomy vs. Protection:** Geofencing restricts movement; balance against the patient's right to "reasonable risk."
4. **Data Security:** Health and location data must comply with **DPDP Act (India)**, **HIPAA (US)**, or **GDPR (EU)** — encrypted transmission, limited access.
5. **Stigmatization:** Visible devices may label the user; discreet form factors (watches, brooches) are preferable.
6. **Use of Least Restrictive Alternative:** GPS is preferred over locked doors or physical restraints.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing P(Y) with P-code only** — P-code is encrypted to Y-code for anti-spoofing; do not call it "P-code only."
> 2. **Forgetting that 3 satellites give a 2D fix** (with assumed altitude) and **4 give a 3D fix** — write both.
> 3. **Mixing up the orbit type** — GPS satellites are in **MEO** (not GEO, not LEO). IGSO/GEO are used by **NavIC/IRNSS**, not GPS.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — pseudorange is in **meters**, clock bias is converted to **meters** ($b = c \cdot \delta t$).
> 5. **Skipping the "why four satellites"** explanation — examiners expect to see the role of the clock bias $b$ explicitly.
> 6. **Missing the DOP definition** in numerical form — write $\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{q_{11}+q_{22}+q_{33}}$ at least once.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **GPS = USA satellite navigation system** providing PVT to passive receivers.
- [x] **Three segments:** Space (31 MEO satellites, 20,200 km, 6 planes, 55° inclination), Control (MCS + 6 monitors + 4 uplinks), User (passive receivers).
- [x] **L1 = 1575.42 MHz** (civilian C/A + military P(Y) + nav message).
- [x] **L2 = 1227.60 MHz** (P(Y) + nav message).
- [x] **L5 = 1176.45 MHz** (modernized civilian, Safety-of-Life).
- [x] **Pseudorange** $\rho_i = R_i + b$ contains an unknown clock bias $b$ in meters.
- [x] **Four satellites** are required for 3D fix (3 for position + 1 for time/bias).
- [x] **Trilateration** (not triangulation) — based on distance, not angles.
- [x] **C/A code** = 1023 chips, 1.023 Mcps, 1 ms period, civilian.
- [x] **P(Y) code** = 10.23 Mcps, encrypted, military.
- [x] **Navigation message** = 50 bps, 12.5 min total, contains ephemeris, almanac, clock corrections.
- [x] **DOP** quantifies geometry effect; **PDOP ≤ 4** is good.
- [x] **UERE ≈ 5.3 m (1σ)** — typical SPS error budget.
- [x] **Multipath** is the dominant indoor error; mitigated by choke-ring or polarized antennas.
- [x] **DGPS** uses a base station to broadcast range corrections → accuracy 1–3 m.
- [x] **A-GPS** uses cellular assistance for faster TTFF and better indoor sensitivity — ideal for rehabilitation wearables.
- [x] **Rehabilitation applications:** wandering detection, way-finding, SOS, activity monitoring, geofencing.
- [x] **Ethical framework:** privacy, informed consent, autonomy, data security (DPDP/GDPR/HIPAA).
- [x] **Comparison anchors:** GLONASS (Russia, 64.8°), Galileo (EU, < 1 m), NavIC (India, regional GEO+IGSO).
- [x] **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the basis of all ranging.
- [x] **Earth radius** $R_E = 6371$ km — used for ECEF ↔ geodetic conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audible and Remotely Readable Signs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Audible and Remotely Readable Signs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Audible Signs — Formal Definition

**Audible Signs** are assistive communication devices that convey spatial, navigational, or informational cues to individuals through **acoustic signals** (tones, chirps, melodic patterns, recorded speech, or synthesized voice). They form a critical subset of **Universal Design** infrastructure mandated under accessibility codes (ADA, RPZ, ISO 7001, MUTCD) and serve users with **visual impairment, cognitive disability, or low literacy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEEBT834):** Audible signs are classified into two principal technological families:
> 1. **Audio Beacons / APS (Audible Pedestrian Signals)** — fixed-location tone emitters.
> 2. **Talking Signs (Remote Infrared Audible Signage — RIAS)** — directionally-encoded, IR-modulated speech units activated by a handheld receiver.

## 1.2 Remotely Readable Signs — Formal Definition

**Remotely Readable Signs** are visual or multimodal information displays engineered so that a user can perceive and decode their content **without approaching the sign or making tactile contact**. They are characterized by:

- **Legibility distance ≥ 3 m** (under standard ambient lighting of **100 lux**),
- **High luminance contrast ratio ≥ 7:1** (preferred 10:1) per WCAG/ADAAG,
- **Character height ≥ 75 mm** at 3 m, scaling linearly,
- Optional **electronic / wireless augmentation** (BLE beacons, NFC, QR, IR) for assistive devices.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Talking Street Corner"**
> Imagine standing at a busy intersection. A sighted person reads the street sign. An audible sign is like a small **invisible announcer** standing on the corner, softly saying *"Pharmacy ahead, 15 metres"* whenever your phone or receiver comes within range. A remotely readable sign is like a **loud, oversized billboard** with huge, high-contrast letters — visible from across the street without having to walk up to it. Together, they form **two sensory channels** (ear + eye) for the same information — the heart of **redundant accessibility design**.

## 1.4 Categorical Taxonomy

| Sensory Channel | Class | Example Device | Primary User |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Auditory | Audio Beacon | Pedestrian chirp at crosswalk | Blind pedestrian |
| Auditory | Talking Sign (IR) | Smith-Kettlewell RIAS | Visually impaired traveller |
| Auditory | Audio Announcement | Escalator / lift verbal call | All users |
| Visual | Large-print Sign | Hospital corridor board | Low-vision user |
| Visual | High-contrast Sign | Tactile-raised + bold | Low-vision / elderly |
| Electronic | BLE / NFC Beacon | Indoor wayfinding app | Smart-device user |
| Multimodal | Sign + Beacon + Braille | Accessible washroom sign | All PWDs |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Audible Pedestrian Signals (APS)

An APS is a **fixed-location tone generator** mounted on a pedestrian signal pole. During the WALK interval, it emits a **rapidly repeating tone** whose **pitch, repetition rate, and direction-of-arrival** encode which crossing is active.

**Standardized sound conventions (per MUTCD / NCHRP):**

- **North–South walk** → 880 Hz *cuckoo* (3 repeats, ~5 Hz chirp burst).
- **East–West walk** → 880 Hz *chirp–chirp* (5–7 bursts, ~9 Hz).
- **Locator tone** → continuous 880 Hz, ~0.5 s pulse every 1.0 s, during the DON'T WALK state (for orientation only).
- **Speech message mode** → 5 dB above ambient, 2-second max, multilingual.

**Design parameter — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):**

$$L_{\text{signal}} \geq L_{\text{noise}} + 5\ \text{dB}$$

with the absolute ceiling $L_{\text{signal,max}} = 89\ \text{dB SPL}$ at **1 m** from the speaker, calibrated against ambient noise measured at the curb extension.

## 2.2 Talking Sign (RIAS) System — Theory of Operation

The **Remote Infrared Audible Signage (RIAS)** system, developed at the *Smith-Kettlewell Eye Research Institute*, consists of two subsystems:

1. **Transmitter module** — Sign-mounted IR LED array emitting a **narrow IR beam (typically 875 nm or 940 nm)** modulated at a carrier frequency of **38 kHz ± 500 Hz**. The audio message is encoded via **pulse-width modulation (PWM)** or **PPM (pulse-position modulation)**.
2. **Handheld receiver** — Worn by the user; contains a wide-field **photodiode / phototransistor**, a 38 kHz bandpass filter, a demodulator, a low-power audio amplifier, and a miniature speaker or earpiece.

The user **points** the receiver toward the sign; the directional IR beam guarantees **angle-of-arrival discrimination**, which a diffuse audio source cannot provide. Typical operating range is **6 m to 9 m** for a handheld unit, extendable to **30 m** with a higher-gain antenna.

## 2.3 Remotely Readable Sign — Legibility Theory

Legibility distance $D_L$ of a sign is governed by **Snellen-style visual acuity** combined with environmental factors. The **International Standards Organization (ISO 21542)** and **ANSI A117.1** specify:

$$D_L = K \cdot H_c \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (E_b/E_{\text{ref}})}$$

where $K \approx 1.6$ (constant), $H_c$ = cap-height of characters (mm), $E_b$ = background luminance, $E_{\text{ref}} = 100$ lux.

For tactile-augmented remotely readable signs, **character height ratio** between raised text and baseline must be $\geq 1:5$ to allow fingertip discrimination by users with residual vision.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ reference |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | Inverse Square Law (free field) | $I(r) = I_0 (r_0 / r)^2$ | Point source assumption |
| 4 | SPL with distance (free field) | $L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}(r/r_0)$ | dB; **6 dB per doubling** |
| 5 | SPL with distance (hemispherical) | $L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 10 \log_{10}(r/r_0)$ | dB; **3 dB per doubling** |
| 6 | Free-Space Path Loss (IR/RF) | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ | $d$ in m, $f$ in Hz |
| 7 | Wavelength from frequency | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ |
| 8 | Coverage cone radius (IR) | $R_{\text{cone}} = d \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$ | $\theta$ = half-power beam angle |
| 9 | Coverage area of cone | $A_{\text{cone}} = \pi R_{\text{cone}}^2$ | m² |
| 10 | ADA required SNR | $L_{\text{signal}} \geq L_{\text{noise}} + 5$ | dB; max **89 dB** at 1 m |
| 11 | Sabine reverberation time | $T_{60} = 0.161\, V / A$ | $V$ in m³, $A$ in sabins |
| 12 | Speech Transmission Index (rule) | $\text{STI} \geq 0.50$ for good intelligibility | 0–1 scale |
| 13 | Legibility distance (ISO 21542) | $D_L = 1.6\, H_c / (1 + E_b/E_{\text{ref}})$ | $H_c$ cap height, mm |
| 14 | Contrast ratio | $\text{CR} = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/L_{\min}$ | ADAAG requires $\geq 70\%$ |
| 15 | IR carrier frequency (RIAS) | $f_c = 38\ \text{kHz} \pm 0.5\ \text{kHz}$ | Modulated speech channel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Audible signs are deployed in **transit hubs (Kerala State Road Transport Corporation bus terminals, Kochi Metro stations), educational institutions, and smart-city projects** under the *Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan)*. Talking Sign systems are used in hospitals like *LV Prasad Eye Institute* and in IATA-compliant airports worldwide. Remotely readable signage forms the cornerstone of **BCA 2016 (India) Chapter 8 — Building Services, Part 3 — Accessibility** and the **RPwD Act 2016**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverse Square Law for Sound Propagation

A pedestrian signal speaker radiates acoustic power $P$ uniformly in all directions. At distance $r$, this power is distributed over the surface of a sphere of area $S = 4\pi r^{2}$. The intensity (power per unit area) is:

$$I(r) = \frac{P}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

Taking the ratio of intensities at two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$\frac{I(r_2)}{I(r_1)} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}$$

In decibels:

$$L_{I}(r_2) - L_{I}(r_1) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I(r_2)}{I(r_1)}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{2}$$

$$= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$

Rearranging with reference $r_0 = 1\ \text{m}$:

$$L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}(r) \quad \text{[free field]}$$

**Result:** SPL drops by **6.02 dB** for every doubling of distance (a memorizable rule of thumb).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free-Space Path Loss for an IR Talking Sign

For an isotropic radiator in free space, the Friis transmission equation gives received power:

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$

The free-space path loss in dB:

$$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_r}\right)\bigg|_{G_t=G_r=1} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

Substituting $\lambda = c / f$:

$$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d f / c)$$

For $d$ in **metres** and $f$ in **Hz**, with $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:

$$20 \log_{10}(4\pi / c) = 20 \log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8}) = -147.55\ \text{dB}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55}$$

**Numerical evaluation for a Talking Sign:**
- IR wavelength: $\lambda = 875\ \text{nm} \Rightarrow f = c/\lambda = 3.43 \times 10^{14}\ \text{Hz}$
- $20\log_{10}(f) = 20 \log_{10}(3.43 \times 10^{14}) = 20(14.535) = 290.7$ dB
- At $d = 6\ \text{m}$: $20 \log_{10}(6) = 15.56$ dB

$$\text{FSPL}_{6\text{m}} = 15.56 + 290.7 - 147.55 = 158.71\ \text{dB}$$

**Interpretation:** To overcome ~159 dB of path loss, the transmitter uses a **high-power IR LED cluster** (peak radiant intensity $I_e \geq 100\ \text{mW/sr}$) and a **narrow optical beam** (half-angle $\theta \leq 15°$). This trade-off between range and directionality is the defining engineering challenge of RIAS.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coverage Cone of an IR Talking Sign

A directional IR source projects a conical beam. The half-power beam angle is $\theta$. At distance $d$, the cone footprint is a circle of radius:

$$R_{\text{cone}} = d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Area of coverage:

$$A_{\text{cone}} = \pi R_{\text{cone}}^{2} = \pi d^{2} \tan^{2}\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Numerical example:** $\theta = 30°$, $d = 5\ \text{m}$:

$$R_{\text{cone}} = 5 \times \tan(15°) = 5 \times 0.2679 = 1.34\ \text{m}$$

$$A_{\text{cone}} = \pi (1.34)^{2} = 5.64\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Insight:** A 30° cone covers a 1.34 m radius at 5 m — enough for a single pedestrian to be unambiguously "inside" the beam, but tight enough to discriminate the sign from neighbouring signs across the corridor.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SPL Drop Along a Crosswalk

An APS at 89 dB SPL at 1 m is mounted at the far end of a 6 m crosswalk. Find SPL at the curb where the user stands.

$$L_p(6) = L_p(1) - 20 \log_{10}(6/1)$$

$$L_p(6) = 89 - 20 \log_{10}(6) = 89 - 15.56 = 73.44\ \text{dB}$$

If ambient traffic noise is $L_N = 70$ dB, then SNR at the user:

$$\text{SNR} = L_p - L_N = 73.44 - 70 = 3.44\ \text{dB}$$

**This is below the ADA requirement of 5 dB.** The fix: the volume must be raised, OR the **locator tone** at the curb-side speaker must be increased, OR a second speaker must be added at the near end.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Audible vs. Remotely Readable Signs

| Parameter | Audible Sign (Audio Beacon) | Talking Sign (IR) | Remotely Readable Sign (Visual) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Information density | Low (tone / chirp) | High (full speech) | Medium (text + pictogram) |
| Directionality | Omnidirectional | Highly directional | Mid-directional (sight-line) |
| Range | 5–10 m | 6–30 m | 3–30 m (legibility-based) |
| Power source | Mains / solar | Battery receiver | Mains / passive (printed) |
| Privacy concern | Low | Medium (anyone with receiver) | None |
| User cognitive load | Very low | Low | Low–medium |
| Best fit for | Open public spaces | Confined corridors, transit | Building entries, directories |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Talking Sign (RIAS) System

```mermaid
graph LR
    MSG[Message Source: Pre-recorded speech] --> ENC[Audio Encoder PWM or PPM]
    MOD[38 kHz Modulator] --> IRDRIVE[IR LED Driver]
    ENC --> MOD
    IRDRIVE --> IREMIT[IR LED Array 875 nm or 940 nm]
    IREMIT --> BEAM[Directional IR Beam 15 to 30 deg cone]
    BEAM --> PDIODE[Photodiode at Handheld Receiver]
    PDIODE --> BPF[Bandpass Filter 38 kHz center]
    BPF --> DEMOD[Demodulator and Decoder]
    DEMOD --> AMP[Audio Amplifier low power]
    AMP --> SPKOUT[Earpiece or Miniature Speaker]
    PDIODE --> BAT[Receiver Battery 9V typical]
    PWRMAINS[DC Mains or Solar] --> IRDRIVE
    USER[User with Visual Impairment] --> SPKOUT
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Audio Pedestrian Signal (APS) with Locator + Walk Tones

```mermaid
graph LR
    TRL[Traffic Signal Controller] --> ACT[Walk Interval Trigger]
    ACT --> GEN1[880 Hz Locator Tone Generator]
    ACT --> GEN2[Cuckoo Chirp Generator N-S]
    ACT --> GEN3[Chirp Generator E-W]
    ACT --> GEN4[Speech Module Optional Multilingual]
    GEN1 --> MIX[Audio Mixer]
    GEN2 --> MIX
    GEN3 --> MIX
    GEN4 --> MIX
    MIX --> AMP[Class-D Amplifier]
    AMP --> SPK1[Speaker Pole-Mounted N-S Pole]
    AMP --> SPK2[Speaker Pole-Mounted E-W Pole]
    MIC[Ambient Noise Microphone at Curb] --> AVC[Automatic Volume Control Loop]
    AVC --> AMP
    AVC --> SENSE[5 dB above ambient up to 89 dB at 1 m]
    PWR[Mains 230 V AC 50 Hz] --> AMP
```

## 4.3 Multi-Modal Remotely Readable Sign Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    INFO[Source Information: Room Name, Function] --> REND[Renderer: Layout Engine]
    REND --> TXT[High-Contrast Bold Text 75 mm cap-height]
    REND --> PICTO[Pictogram ISO 7001 Standard]
    REND --> BR[Braille Tactile Raised Dots]
    REND --> NFP[NFC Passive Tag Embedded]
    REND --> BLE[BLE Beacon Battery 1 to 3 yr]
    TXT --> PRINT[Printed Substrate Vinyl or Acrylic]
    PICTO --> PRINT
    BR --> PRINT
    NFP --> ENCAP[Encapsulated Behind Acrylic]
    BLE --> ENCAP
    USER1[Sighted User] --> TXT
    USER2[Low-Vision User] --> TXT
    USER3[Blind User] --> BR
    USER4[Smartphone User] --> NFP
    USER5[App User Indoor Navigation] --> BLE
```

## 4.4 Crosswalk Deployment Geometry

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Building Corner with Signpost] --> B[APS Speaker N-S Direction]
    A --> C[APS Speaker E-W Direction]
    B --> D[Pedestrian Curb Extension 1.8 m]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Crosswalk 6 m wide]
    E --> F[Opposite Curb with Locator Tone]
    F --> G[Talking Sign on Opposite Building: Pharmacy 15 m]
    USER[Visually Impaired User at D] -->|Listens chirp| B
    USER -->|Listens chirp| C
    USER -->|Points handheld receiver| G
    AMB[Ambient Microphone 70 dB] --> AVC[AVC: Boost to 75 dB 5 dB above]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define audible pedestrian signals. List any two design parameters specified by ADA standards for APS installations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Audible Pedestrian Signals (APS) are fixed-location tone-emitting devices mounted on pedestrian signal poles that provide **acoustic cues** indicating the WALK interval to pedestrians with visual impairment. They supplement visual pedestrian signals by encoding **direction** (e.g., cuckoo tone for north–south, chirp–chirp for east–west) and **interval phase** (WALK vs. DON'T WALK) into the audio signal.

**Two ADA design parameters:**
1. **Sound level:** $L_{\text{signal}} = L_{\text{noise,ambient}} + 5\ \text{dB}$, with an absolute maximum of **89 dB SPL at 1 m** from the speaker.
2. **Locator tone:** A continuous 880 Hz tone pulsed approximately once per second during the DON'T WALK phase, helping the user locate the pole and align for crossing.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — direction encoding; 1 mark — two ADA parameters with values.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**What is a Talking Sign system? State the modulation carrier frequency and the typical operating range of the handheld receiver.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Talking Sign (Remote Infrared Audible Signage / RIAS)** is a wireless accessibility system in which a sign-mounted IR transmitter encodes a pre-recorded speech message onto a directional infrared beam. A handheld receiver carried by the visually impaired user decodes the IR signal and plays the corresponding audio, allowing the user to identify and locate the sign from a distance.

- **Modulation carrier frequency:** $f_c = 38\ \text{kHz} \pm 0.5\ \text{kHz}$ (standard IR remote control band).
- **Typical operating range of handheld receiver:** **6 m to 9 m**, extendable up to **30 m** for higher-gain receivers.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — carrier frequency; 1 mark — range.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Do **not** confuse "Talking Sign" (IR-based, directional) with generic "audio signage" — students lose 1–2 marks for this conflation.
> - Always quote **numerical standards** (e.g., 89 dB at 1 m, 38 kHz, 5 dB above ambient) — vague qualitative answers fetch only 50% credit.
> - In derivation questions, **state assumptions** (point source, free-field, isotropic) — unmarked assumptions cost at least 1 mark per derivation.
> - Block diagrams must include **functional blocks with arrows showing signal flow** — unlabeled blocks get zero credit for the diagram portion.

### Question 3 (a) — Choice A [14 Marks Total: 7 + 7]

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the various types of audible pedestrian signals (APS) used for visually impaired pedestrians. Discuss the ADA standards governing sound level, locator tone, and tone direction convention. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Audible Pedestrian Signals (APS) can be categorized by the **type of audio cue** they produce:

1. **Tone-only APS (Cuckoo/Chirp model):**
   - North–South WALK → 880 Hz *cuckoo* (3 bursts per cycle).
   - East–West WALK → 880 Hz *chirp–chirp* (5–7 bursts per cycle).
   - Pulses repeat at **5–10 Hz repetition rate** during WALK.
   - Direction discrimination is based on **sound localization** (timbre difference).

2. **Speech-message APS:**
   - Plays a recorded phrase like *"Walk sign is on, north–south"* during the WALK phase.
   - Limited to **2-second** phrases for safety.
   - Multilingual versions used in India (English, Hindi, Malayalam, Tamil).

3. **Locator-tone APS:**
   - Continuously emits a **short 880 Hz tick** every **1.0 s** during the DON'T WALK phase.
   - Helps the user locate the pole before walking.
   - Volume auto-adjusted to ambient noise (AVC loop).

**ADA Standards (per NCHRP 3-62 & MUTCD 2009):**
- **Sound level:** $L_{\text{signal}} = L_{\text{noise}} + 5\ \text{dB}$, max **89 dB at 1 m**.
- **Locator tone:** at curb, audible at **1.8 m to 3.7 m** from the pole.
- **Frequency:** 880 Hz primary, with harmonics extending to ~2 kHz for localization cues.
- **Speech messages:** only on user request (pushbutton activation) to avoid noise pollution.

*(3 marks — types; 2 marks — tone-direction convention with frequency/repetition; 2 marks — ADA numerical standards.)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Talking Sign (RIAS) system. Discuss why IR (rather than RF) is used as the carrier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (refer Section 4.1):**

The system has two ends:

**Transmitter end:**
- **Message source** → pre-recorded speech (e.g., "Pharmacy, 15 metres ahead").
- **Audio encoder** → PWM/PPM modulation of the audio waveform.
- **38 kHz modulator** → mixes audio onto IR carrier.
- **IR LED driver** → high-current pulsed driver.
- **IR LED array (875 nm or 940 nm)** → emits narrow directional beam.

**Receiver end (handheld):**
- **Photodiode** → wide-FOV detector.
- **38 kHz bandpass filter** → rejects ambient light.
- **Demodulator/decoder** → recovers audio baseband.
- **Audio amplifier (low-power Class-AB)** → drives earpiece.
- **Earpiece** → delivers speech to user.

**Working principle:** The user **points** the handheld receiver toward the sign. The narrow IR beam (half-angle 15°–30°) ensures that only the sign being pointed at is decoded. As the user rotates, a different sign's IR beam aligns, and the corresponding message is heard — providing **direction-of-arrival information** that audio alone cannot.

**Why IR (not RF)?**
1. **Privacy:** IR does not penetrate walls; the signal is contained to the line-of-sight volume, preventing eavesdropping in adjacent rooms — critical in hospitals and banks.
2. **Spectrum licensing:** IR is unregulated; RF (e.g., 2.4 GHz) requires compliance with national spectrum allocation.
3. **Safety:** IR at the powers used is **eye-safe** (Class 1 LED) under IEC 60825.
4. **Directionality:** IR beams can be tightly focused with simple optics; RF diffracts.
5. **No EMI interference:** IR is immune to RF jamming and to medical equipment EMI.

*(2 marks — transmitter block diagram; 2 marks — receiver block diagram + signal flow; 2 marks — working principle; 1 mark — five reasons for IR choice.)*

---

### Question 4 (b) — Choice B (Internal Choice Alternative) [14 Marks Total: 7 + 7]

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the design principles and accessibility standards for **remotely readable signs** for visually impaired users. Cover character height, contrast ratio, finish, and placement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Remotely readable signs are designed to be **legible from a distance** without tactile contact. The design principles are codified in:

- **ADA Standards (2010)**, section **703**,
- **ANSI A117.1**,
- **ISO 21542** (Building construction — Accessibility),
- **RPwD Act 2016 + Harmonised Guidelines (India)**.

**1. Character height:**
- For signs viewed from **3 m**: cap height $\geq$ **75 mm**.
- For every additional **1.5 m** of viewing distance, increase cap height by **25 mm**.

**2. Contrast ratio (CR):**
- Foreground-to-background luminance ratio must be $\geq 70\%$ (preferred $\geq 80\%$).
- Formula: $\text{CR} = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/L_{\min}$.
- Best contrast: **white-on-black** or **yellow-on-black**.

**3. Finish / Material:**
- **Matte (non-glare)** finish; specular gloss $\leq 12$ GU at 60° (ISO 2813).
- Avoid embossed metallic surfaces that create glare.

**4. Font and stroke width:**
- **Sans-serif** fonts (e.g., Helvetica, Arial) preferred; **stroke-width-to-character-height** ratio $\geq 1:5$ for legibility.
- Avoid all-caps for body text; mixed-case is more legible.

**5. Placement:**
- Mount at **1.4 m to 1.6 m** above finished floor (centre of sign).
- Unobstructed sight-line from a **3 m approach distance**.
- **Uniform location** across a building (consistency rule) so users can predict sign location.

**6. Pictograms:**
- Conformance to **ISO 7001** public information symbols.
- Pictogram height equal to cap height.

*(2 marks — character height & placement numbers; 2 marks — contrast ratio formula and standard; 2 marks — finish & font; 1 mark — pictogram & ISO reference.)*

**(b)** Explain the role of **beacon-based assistive navigation systems** (BLE, NFC) in supporting accessible wayfinding. Compare them with audio beacons and Talking Signs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Beacon-based navigation systems** are electronic aids that broadcast a unique identifier wirelessly to a smartphone or assistive device carried by the user. The two principal types are:

**1. BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) Beacons (e.g., iBeacon, Eddystone):**
- Operate in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**.
- Broadcast a **UUID + Major + Minor** identifier at **100 ms to 1 s** intervals.
- Range: **3 m to 70 m** depending on transmit power.
- Power: coin-cell battery (1–3 years).
- Application: indoor wayfinding, transit navigation, museum guides.

**2. NFC (Near-Field Communication) Tags:**
- Operate at **13.56 MHz** with a range of **≤ 10 cm** (tap-to-read).
- Passive tags (no battery) powered by the reader's RF field.
- Storage: up to **8 KB** for URL, text, or app-launcher data.
- Application: bus-stop information, room identifiers, payment.

**3. Comparison with Audio Beacons & Talking Signs:**

| Feature | Audio Beacon | Talking Sign (IR) | BLE/NFC Beacon |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Information richness | Very low | High (full speech) | Medium (text/URL) |
| Receiver needed | None (passive ear) | Handheld IR receiver | Smartphone |
| Privacy | High (open) | Medium | High (user opt-in) |
| Power | Mains | Battery + mains | Battery / passive |
| Direction-of-arrival | Approximate (timbre) | Highly accurate | Approximate (RSSI) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Low per tag, high app dev |

**Key engineering insight:** BLE is **omnidirectional and non-discriminating** (multiple beacons received at once); the user app must rank beacons by **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)**. Talking Sign's IR cone is **naturally directional**, giving an unambiguous single-sign decode without an algorithm. Hence IR remains the gold-standard for sign-level precision, while BLE is preferred for **building-level** navigation.

*(2 marks — BLE explanation with parameters; 2 marks — NFC explanation; 2 marks — comparison table; 1 mark — engineering insight on directionality.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Audible Pedestrian Signal (APS):** 880 Hz cuckoo = N–S; chirp = E–W; ADA max **89 dB at 1 m**; SNR ≥ **5 dB above ambient**.
- [x] **Talking Sign (RIAS):** IR **38 kHz** carrier, wavelength **875 nm / 940 nm**, range **6–9 m** handheld, **30 m** extended; uses PWM/PPM encoding.
- [x] **Why IR (not RF)?** Privacy, eye-safety, spectrum-free, directional, no EMI.
- [x] **Inverse Square Law (free field):** SPL drops **6 dB per doubling of distance**; $L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}(r/r_0)$.
- [x] **FSPL formula (IR/RF):** $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ (d in m, f in Hz).
- [x] **Coverage cone of IR sign:** $A_{\text{cone}} = \pi d^{2} \tan^{2}(\theta/2)$.
- [x] **Remotely readable sign:** Cap height **≥ 75 mm** at 3 m; contrast **≥ 70%**; matte finish; mount at **1.4–1.6 m** AFFL; ISO 7001 pictograms.
- [x] **BLE vs. NFC vs. Talking Sign:** BLE for building-level, NFC for tap-to-read, Talking Sign for sign-level precision.
- [x] **Speech intelligibility:** **STI ≥ 0.50** is acceptable; reverberation time $T_{60} = 0.161\,V/A$.
- [x] **Rehabilitation context:** All these systems are mandated by the **RPwD Act 2016, BCA 2016, Accessible India Campaign, ADA 2010, ISO 21542**.
- [x] **Common KTU pitfall:** Confusing locator tone (continuous, 1 Hz pulse) with WALK tone (rapid burst). Know the difference.
- [x] **Numericals to practice:** SPL drop with distance; FSPL for IR; coverage area; SNR check against ADA 5 dB rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute examiner tip:** When a question asks "Discuss audible signs," always structure your answer into three blocks: **(1) Principle of operation with frequency/pitch convention, (2) Engineering specifications with numerical standards, (3) Block diagram of the system.** KTU examiners award marks block-wise — missing one block can cost up to **3–4 marks** out of 7.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neural Repair and Rehabilitation - Outcome Measurement and Diagnostic Technology - Electromyography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT834 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neural Repair and Rehabilitation - Outcome Measurement and Diagnostic Technology - Electromyography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromyography (EMG)** is an electrodiagnostic medical technique that records the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles, used to evaluate the functional integrity of the **motor unit** (an alpha motor neuron, its axon, and all the muscle fibers it innervates). It is one of the most important **Outcome Measurement and Diagnostic Technologies** in neural repair and rehabilitation engineering.

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering (OEEBT834)**, EMG serves three primary diagnostic functions aligned with KTU Module 4 outcomes:

1. **Diagnostic evaluation** of neuromuscular disorders (e.g., ALS, peripheral neuropathy, myopathies, radiculopathy).
2. **Functional assessment** as a quantitative **outcome measure** to track neural recovery post-stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI), or prosthetic fitting.
3. **Control signal generation** for assistive devices (powered prosthetics, exoskeletons, FES systems).

### 1.1.1 The Motor Unit – The Fundamental Unit of EMG

The **motor unit** is the smallest functional contractile unit that can be independently activated by the nervous system. It consists of:

- A single **alpha motor neuron** (lower motor neuron in the anterior horn of the spinal cord).
- Its **axon** and axonal branches.
- The **neuromuscular junctions (motor end plates)**.
- All **muscle fibers** innervated by that single axon (typically 5–2000 fibers depending on the muscle's precision requirement).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The size principle (Henneman's size principle) governs motor unit recruitment — smaller motor units (Type I, slow-twitch) are recruited first for low-force tasks, and larger motor units (Type II, fast-twitch) are recruited for high-force tasks. EMG signals are a spatial-temporal summation of multiple motor unit action potentials (MUAPs).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Microphone for Muscles" Analogy

Imagine a **large stadium filled with thousands of people** (the muscle fibers). The **team coach** (the brain) sends a single assistant coach (the motor neuron) to organize a section of the crowd to cheer. When the coach whispers instructions through the assistant, only that section cheers. If you placed a **microphone** above the stadium, you would hear the **combined cheer of just that section** — a small, organized noise.

An **EMG electrode is essentially a microphone listening to the electrical "cheers" of muscle fibers** as their controlling motor neurons fire. By analyzing this electrical "noise," a rehabilitation engineer or neurologist can determine:

- **Is the coach (brain/spinal cord) sending messages?** (Central control intact?)
- **Is the assistant coach (motor neuron) healthy?** (Lower motor neuron disease?)
- **Are the people (muscle fibers) listening and cheering back?** (Neuromuscular junction integrity, e.g., Myasthenia Gravis?)
- **How big is the section being recruited?** (Motor unit size — diagnostic of neuropathy vs. myopathy.)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Tuning a Radio:** A healthy EMG signal is like a clear radio station with proper frequency. **Neuropathic conditions** produce "static" (fibrillations, positive sharp waves) when the nerve is damaged. **Myopathic conditions** produce a "weak, short signal" (small, polyphasic MUAPs) when muscle fibers are lost. EMG decodes these patterns just like an audio engineer tunes a radio.

---

## 1.3 EMG Signal Characteristics — Key Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **MUAP Duration** | **5–15 ms** | Indicates motor unit territory; increases in neuropathy, decreases in myopathy |
| **MUAP Amplitude** | **200 µV – 2 mV** (surface); up to **5 mV** (needle) | Reflects fiber density near electrode |
| **EMG Bandwidth** | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** (clinical); up to **5 kHz** (research) | Dominant energy is in 50–150 Hz range |
| **Recruitment Frequency** | **5–15 Hz** (onset) | Firing rate of motor units |
| **Skin-Electrode Impedance** | **< 5 kΩ** (recommended) | Critical for signal quality |
| **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)** | **> 90 dB** | Rejection of environmental noise |

---

## 1.4 Types of EMG — Two Principal Modalities

### 1.4.1 Surface EMG (sEMG) — Non-Invasive

- Electrodes placed on the skin surface over the muscle belly.
- Detects the **superimposed activity of multiple motor units** within ~1–2 cm radius.
- **Application:** Prosthetic control, gait analysis, biofeedback, ergonomics, sports rehabilitation.

### 1.4.2 Intramuscular EMG (iEMG / Needle EMG) — Invasive

- Concentric or monopolar needle inserted directly into the muscle.
- Records activity from a **small, localized group of muscle fibers** (the "needle's pick-up territory").
- **Application:** Clinical diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders, motor unit number estimation (MUNE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Mapping:** Outcome measurement in neural repair requires both modalities — **needle EMG** for diagnostic precision and **surface EMG** for longitudinal, non-invasive tracking of recovery and prosthetic interface.

---

## 1.5 Place of EMG in Neural Repair and Rehabilitation

EMG bridges **diagnosis**, **prognosis**, and **intervention**:

- **Pre-intervention:** Establish baseline motor function before nerve repair surgery, tendon transfer, or prosthetic fitting.
- **During intervention:** Intraoperative EMG (triggered EMG) helps surgeons identify and preserve motor nerves.
- **Post-intervention:** Serial EMG studies quantify **reinnervation** progress — a critical **outcome measure** for nerve repair, tendon transfers, and FES rehabilitation.
- **Long-term follow-up:** Quantitative EMG (QEMG) and motor unit number estimation (MUNE) track chronic conditions like post-polio syndrome or ALS progression.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrophysiological Foundation of the EMG Signal

### 2.1.1 Generation of the Muscle Fiber Action Potential (MFAP)

The EMG signal originates from the depolarization-repolarization cycle of the **sarcolemma** (muscle cell membrane). The sequence is:

1. **Acetylcholine (ACh) release** at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ) — quantal release, ~50–200 vesicles per nerve impulse.
2. **End-plate potential (EPP)** generated — if EPP exceeds threshold, an all-or-none **muscle fiber action potential** propagates along the sarcolemma at ~2–6 m/s.
3. **Propagation** into the **T-tubules** triggers Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum → **excitation-contraction coupling** → muscle contraction.
4. **Repolarization** restores resting membrane potential (~-90 mV inside).

> [!NOTE]
> The **volume conductor** theory describes how the action potential of a single fiber spreads through the surrounding tissue (a conductive medium with resistivity ρ ≈ 300–500 Ω·cm for muscle). The electrode detects this as a triphasic waveform (positive-negative-positive) when the depolarization wavefront passes the recording tip.

### 2.1.2 Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)

A **MUAP** is the **spatially and temporally summed** action potentials of all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit that are within the electrode's pick-up territory (typically ~1 mm for concentric needle, ~2.5 mm for monopolar).

$$\text{MUAP}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MFAP}_i(t - \tau_i)$$

where **N** = number of active fibers within the pick-up territory, and **τᵢ** = temporal dispersion (jitter) due to differing axonal branch lengths and conduction velocities.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Polyphasic"?** When the temporal dispersion (jitter) is large, the MUAP breaks into multiple phases (>4 phases). A normal MUAP has **2–4 phases**; >4 = **polyphasic** (seen in reinnervation and myopathy). The **turns-amplitude** analysis (Willison, 1963) is a classic KTU-recommended quantitative technique.

---

## 2.2 EMG Signal Acquisition Chain

The complete signal chain is:

**Muscle → Electrode → Preamplifier → Main Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → ADC → Digital Processing**

### 2.2.1 Electrode Types (Detailed)

| Electrode Type | Configuration | Bandwidth | Application |
|----------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Surface (Ag/AgCl)** | Bipolar, 2 cm inter-electrode distance | 20–500 Hz | sEMG, prosthetics, biofeedback |
| **Concentric Needle** | Single needle with off-axis wire core | 2 Hz – 10 kHz | Clinical diagnostic EMG |
| **Monopolar Needle** | Single needle + surface reference | 2 Hz – 10 kHz | Single fiber EMG (SFEMG) |
| **Single Fiber Needle** | 25 µm port, 3 mm recording surface | 500 Hz – 20 kHz | Jitter analysis, SFEMG |
| **Fine Wire / Hooked Wire** | Insulated wires in muscle | 20 Hz – 5 kHz | Dynamic movement, gait |
| **High-Density sEMG (HD-EMG)** | 2D grid of surface electrodes | 20–500 Hz | Motor unit decomposition |

### 2.2.2 Skin Preparation and Electrode Placement (sEMG Protocol — SENIAM Guidelines)

1. **Shave** hair from the skin overlying the muscle belly.
2. **Abrade** the skin with fine sandpaper or abrasive paste (removes dead stratum corneum).
3. **Clean** with alcohol wipe; let dry completely.
4. **Apply** Ag/AgCl electrodes along the muscle fiber direction (parallel to fibers), centered on the muscle belly.
5. **Reference electrode** placed on electrically neutral tissue (e.g., bony prominence — patella, olecranon).
6. Check **inter-electrode impedance** < **5 kΩ** at 30 Hz.

---

## 2.3 Signal Processing Architecture

### 2.3.1 Amplification

EMG signals are low-amplitude (µV–mV range) and require amplification. The typical amplification chain:

$$V_{out} = G_{pre} \cdot G_{main} \cdot (V_{diff} + V_{common})$$

where **G_pre** ≈ 10–100, **G_main** ≈ 100–1000, and **V_diff** is the differential signal.

The differential amplifier rejects common-mode signals (e.g., 50/60 Hz powerline interference):

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

A CMRR of **90 dB** means 1/31,623 of common-mode noise is preserved — essential for clinical EMG.

### 2.3.2 Filtering

| Filter | Cutoff | Purpose |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **High-pass** | 20 Hz (sEMG) / 2 Hz (needle) | Remove motion artifact, DC offset |
| **Low-pass (anti-alias)** | 500 Hz (sEMG) / 10 kHz (needle) | Limit bandwidth before sampling |
| **Notch (50/60 Hz)** | 50 Hz / 60 Hz | Remove powerline interference |
| **Sampling rate** | ≥ 1000 Hz (sEMG) / ≥ 20 kHz (needle) | Satisfy Nyquist: f_s ≥ 2·f_max |

### 2.3.3 Rectification and Smoothing (for Envelope Extraction)

To get the **EMG envelope** (linear envelope), the raw signal is processed as:

$$x_{rect}(t) = \vert x(t) \vert \quad \text{(full-wave rectification)}$$

$$x_{env}(t) = \text{LPF}\{x_{rect}(t)\} \quad \text{(typically 4th-order Butterworth, cutoff 6–10 Hz)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** is the most common amplitude estimator in modern EMG analysis, replacing older integrated EMG (iEMG) methods.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formulas for KTU ESE — memorize with units and conditions**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Condition / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Differential Amplifier Output** | $V_{out} = A_d (V_{+} - V_{-}) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_{+}+V_{-}}{2}\right)$ | V | $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| 2 | **CMRR** | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)$ | dB | Should be > 90 dB for clinical EMG |
| 3 | **MUAP as Sum of MFAPs** | $\text{MUAP}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MFAP}_i(t - \tau_i)$ | mV | Temporal summation of fiber APs |
| 4 | **Conduction Velocity (muscle fiber)** | $CV = \frac{d}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | 2–6 m/s; measured with two electrodes |
| 5 | **RMS Amplitude** | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt}$ | mV | Preferred amplitude estimator |
| 6 | **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** | $\text{MAV} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \vert x(t) \vert\, dt$ | mV | Simpler, less robust than RMS |
| 7 | **Integrated EMG (iEMG)** | $\text{iEMG} = \int_0^T \vert x(t) \vert\, dt$ | mV·s | Historical; replaced by RMS |
| 8 | **Median Frequency (f_med)** | $\int_0^{f_{med}} P(f)\,df = \int_{f_{med}}^{\infty} P(f)\,df$ | Hz | Fatigue indicator (decreases with fatigue) |
| 9 | **Mean Power Frequency (MPF)** | $\text{MPF} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} f\cdot P(f)\,df}{\int_0^{\infty} P(f)\,df}$ | Hz | Fatigue indicator; muscle fiber typing |
| 10 | **Nyquist Criterion** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling rate |
| 11 | **SNR** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | > 20 dB required for clinical use |
| 12 | **Inter-electrode Impedance** | $Z \leq 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Ω | At 30 Hz; < 10% imbalance |
| 13 | **Motor Unit Number Estimate (MUNE)** | $\text{MUNE} = \frac{\text{Single MUAP amplitude}}{\text{Compound muscle AP amplitude}}$ | dimensionless | Decreases in motor neuron disease |
| 14 | **Recruitment Ratio** | $\text{Recruitment Ratio} = \frac{\text{Firing Rate}}{\text{Number of Active Units}}$ | Hz/unit | Normally 5–10 Hz/unit |
| 15 | **Henneman Size Principle** | Recruitment order: Type I → Type IIa → Type IIb | — | Smaller motor units recruited first |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Significance and Real-World Utility

### 2.5.1 Clinical Applications
- **Radiculopathy diagnosis:** Denervation fibrillations in paraspinal muscles identify nerve root pathology.
- **Myasthenia Gravis:** Decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS).
- **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome:** Prolonged distal motor and sensory latencies.
- **ALS / Motor Neuron Disease:** Chronic neurogenic changes, large polyphasic MUAPs, reduced recruitment.

### 2.5.2 Rehabilitation Engineering Applications
- **Prosthetic Control:** sEMG signals from residual limb muscles (e.g., biceps/triceps in transhumeral amputees) decode movement intent via pattern recognition (SVM, LDA, CNN).
- **Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES):** EMG-triggered FES uses voluntary EMG to gate stimulation, improving motor relearning post-stroke.
- **Biofeedback Therapy:** Visual/auditory feedback of muscle activity helps patients re-activate weak muscles.
- **Robotic Exoskeletons:** sEMG-based intent detection enables compliant, intention-driven assistance.
- **Wheelchair Control:** sEMG of facial muscles (frontalis) for patients with severe paralysis.

### 2.5.3 Neural Repair Outcome Measurement

| Repair Context | EMG Outcome Measure | Interpretation |
|----------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Peripheral nerve repair** (suture, graft) | Serial MUAP amplitude, recruitment pattern | Reinnervation signs at 3–6 months |
| **Tendon transfer** | New motor activation pattern | Cortical plasticity / re-education |
| **Spinal cord injury** | Below-lesion EMG, MEPs | Central vs. peripheral lesion differentiation |
| **Stroke rehabilitation** | sEMG RMS during functional tasks | Motor recovery quantification (Fugl-Meyer correlation) |
| **Prosthetic fitting** | sEMG pattern consistency | Control signal stability |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMG Amplitude Estimator (RMS) from Statistical Signal Theory

### 3.1.1 Premise

The raw EMG signal $x(t)$ is modeled as a **zero-mean stochastic process** (sum of many independent MUAPs). For such signals, the variance equals the mean square value:

$$\text{Var}[x(t)] = E[x^2(t)] - (E[x(t)])^2 = E[x^2(t)] \quad \text{(since } E[x(t)] = 0\text{)}$$

The variance of an EMG signal corresponds physically to the **signal power** (or intensity) of muscle activation.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of RMS

**Step 1:** Define the **instantaneous power** of the EMG signal at time $t$:

$$p(t) = x^2(t)$$

**Reasoning:** Power is proportional to the square of voltage across a unit resistance ($P = V^2 / R$). Squaring the signal gives the instantaneous power waveform.

**Step 2:** Average this instantaneous power over a finite observation window of duration $T$:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt$$

**Reasoning:** The time-averaged value smooths out the rapid oscillations and gives the average signal power, by the ergodic assumption (time average = ensemble average for stationary EMG).

**Step 3:** Take the square root to obtain the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value, which has the same units as the original signal (volts):

$$\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\bar{P}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt}$$

**Result:** This is the canonical RMS formula. For a discrete sampled EMG signal with $N$ samples:

$$\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2[n]}$$

### 3.1.3 Physical Interpretation

- The **RMS value** represents the **effective voltage** of the EMG signal.
- It correlates strongly with the **force generated by the muscle** (linearly for low to moderate forces, sub-linearly for higher forces due to motor unit synchronization effects).
- A **moving RMS window** (e.g., 100–250 ms) creates a smooth envelope that tracks muscle activation over time.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Example:** A surface EMG signal sampled at 1000 Hz over 1 second yields 1000 samples. Suppose the mean square value is computed as $\bar{x^2} = 0.04\,\text{V}^2$. Then:
> $$\text{RMS} = \sqrt{0.04} = 0.2\,\text{V} = 200\,\text{mV}$$
> This represents a moderate muscle activation level.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) for a Differential Amplifier

### 3.2.1 Setup

A differential amplifier has two inputs $V_+$ and $V_-$. The output is given by the **two-port model**:

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_+ - V_-) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_+ + V_-}{2}\right)$$

where:
- $A_d$ = differential-mode gain (desired)
- $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain (undesired — amplifies noise equally present on both inputs)

### 3.2.2 Express Inputs in Terms of Differential and Common-Mode Components

**Define:**
- Differential component: $V_d = V_+ - V_-$
- Common-mode component: $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_+ + V_-}{2}$

**Step 1:** Inverse transformation — express each input in terms of $V_d$ and $V_{cm}$:

$$V_+ = V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2}, \quad V_- = V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the output equation:

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$$

**Step 3:** The CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential to common-mode gain, in decibels:

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

**Given:** $A_d = 10{,}000$ (differential gain), $A_{cm} = 0.316$ (common-mode gain).

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.316} = 31{,}646$$

**Step 5:** Convert to dB:

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(31{,}646) = 20 \times 4.5004 = 90.0\,\text{dB}$$

**Interpretation:** A 90 dB CMRR means a 1 V common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz interference) appears at the output as only $\frac{1}{31{,}646} \approx 31.6\,\mu V$ — negligible compared to typical EMG amplitudes of 100 µV – 5 mV.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motor Unit Number Estimation (MUNE) Using Incremental Method

### 3.3.1 The McComas Incremental Method (1971)

**Step 1:** Record the **maximum compound muscle action potential (CMAP)** by supramaximally stimulating the motor nerve. Let its peak-to-peak amplitude be $A_{CMAP}$ in mV.

**Step 2:** Apply a series of **graded, incremental stimuli** to the nerve, each at a slightly higher intensity, recruiting one additional motor unit at a time. For each step, measure the **incremental step amplitude** $A_i$ in mV. Each step represents one new motor unit being recruited.

**Step 3:** Identify the **single motor unit action potential (SMUAP)** amplitudes from the incremental steps. The average single motor unit amplitude is:

$$\bar{A}_{MU} = \frac{1}{N_{step}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{step}} A_i$$

where $N_{step}$ is the number of distinct incremental steps observed.

**Step 4:** Estimate the **number of motor units** in the muscle:

$$\text{MUNE} = \frac{A_{CMAP}}{\bar{A}_{MU}}$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Example

**Given:**
- $A_{CMAP} = 8.0\,\text{mV}$ (thenar muscle, supramaximal)
- 10 distinct incremental steps observed, with amplitudes: 0.10, 0.12, 0.08, 0.11, 0.09, 0.13, 0.10, 0.12, 0.09, 0.11 mV.

**Step 5:** Compute the mean single MU amplitude:

$$\bar{A}_{MU} = \frac{0.10 + 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.11 + 0.09 + 0.13 + 0.10 + 0.12 + 0.09 + 0.11}{10} = \frac{1.05}{10} = 0.105\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 6:** Compute MUNE:

$$\text{MUNE} = \frac{8.0}{0.105} \approx 76\,\text{motor units}$$

**Interpretation:** Normal MUNE for the thenar eminence is **~150–200 units**. A value of 76 indicates **~50% motor unit loss**, consistent with early ALS or advanced peripheral neuropathy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The McComas method **assumes all motor units are of similar size** and that incremental steps are well-separated. In practice, "alternation" (variable step amplitudes from the same unit) introduces error. Modern **MUNIX** and **MScanFit** methods are statistically more robust.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — EMG-Based Force Estimation (Linear Envelope Model)

### 3.4.1 Empirical Force-EMG Relationship

For many muscles, the **RMS of sEMG** is approximately linearly related to muscle force during isometric, constant-posture contractions within 0–80% MVC (maximum voluntary contraction):

$$F(t) \approx k \cdot \text{RMS}\{x(t)\} + F_0$$

where:
- $F(t)$ = muscle force (N)
- $\text{RMS}\{x(t)\}$ = moving RMS of EMG (mV)
- $k$ = gain constant (N/mV) — muscle-specific
- $F_0$ = passive force offset (often negligible in voluntary contractions)

### 3.4.2 Derivation from Cross-Correlation Principle

**Step 1:** Model EMG as a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process during a constant-force contraction.

**Step 2:** The signal power (mean square) equals the variance $\sigma^2$ of the process.

**Step 3:** The average rectified value (ARV) is related to the RMS by the form factor of the underlying probability distribution. For Gaussian EMG, ARV = RMS $\cdot \sqrt{2/\pi}$.

**Step 4:** The number of active motor units and their firing rates determine the signal amplitude. Force is proportional to the **sum of twitch tensions** from active units, which is in turn proportional to the **number and rate** of MUAPs.

**Step 5:** Empirically validated linear model:

$$F(t) = \alpha + \beta \cdot \text{RMS}(t)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are obtained by linear regression from a calibration trial.

### 3.4.3 Numerical Calibration Example

**Data from a biceps brachii calibration:**
| Trial | Force F (N) | RMS EMG (mV) |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0.005 |
| 2 | 20 | 0.08 |
| 3 | 50 | 0.21 |
| 4 | 80 | 0.35 |
| 5 | 110 | 0.48 |

**Linear regression yields:** $\alpha = -2.5\,\text{N}$, $\beta = 232\,\text{N/mV}$

**Verification for Trial 5:** $\hat{F} = -2.5 + 232 \times 0.48 = -2.5 + 111.4 = 108.9\,\text{N}$ (measured 110 N, error < 1%)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — EMG Feature Comparison for Outcome Measurement

| Feature | Mathematical Definition | Clinical / Rehab Utility | Limitation |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------|
| **RMS** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x^2}$ | Force estimation, fatigue | Sensitive to noise |
| **MAV** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x \vert$ | Quick amplitude estimate | Less stable than RMS |
| **Zero Crossings (ZC)** | Number of $x=0$ events | Frequency content proxy | Sensitive to noise |
| **Waveform Length (WL)** | $\sum \vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ | Muscle activation complexity | Requires sufficient sampling |
| **Slope Sign Changes (SSC)** | Number of slope reversals | Frequency proxy | Computationally heavy |
| **Median Frequency** | $f_{med}$ from PSD | **Fatigue** monitoring (decreases) | Requires FFT |
| **Mean Frequency (MPF)** | $\frac{\int f P(f) df}{\int P(f) df}$ | Fiber-type composition | Sensitive to noise floor |
| **MUAP Turns** | Number of direction changes | Motor unit territory | Subjective threshold |
| **Motor Unit Firing Rate** | APs per second | Recruitment assessment | Requires decomposition |
| **Co-contraction Index** | $\frac{2 A_{agonist} A_{antagonist}}{A_{agonist}^2 + A_{antagonist}^2}$ | Stroke spasticity assessment | Requires agonist + antagonist |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Fatigue Index from Median Frequency Shift

### 3.6.1 Background

During a sustained isometric contraction, the **median frequency (f_med)** of the EMG power spectral density (PSD) **shifts downward** progressively — this is the classic **spectral compression** of muscle fatigue.

### 3.6.2 Normalized Fatigue Index

$$\text{FI}_{norm} = \frac{f_{med}(t) - f_{med}(0)}{f_{med}(0)}$$

where $f_{med}(0)$ is the initial median frequency and $f_{med}(t)$ is the median frequency at time $t$.

### 3.6.3 Computation Steps

**Step 1:** Acquire EMG during sustained contraction at, e.g., 50% MVC.
**Step 2:** Compute PSD using Welch's method (windowed FFT, e.g., 256-sample windows, 50% overlap).
**Step 3:** Compute cumulative power spectrum; find $f_{med}$ such that 50% of power is below it.
**Step 4:** Track $f_{med}$ across successive time windows.
**Step 5:** Compute normalized fatigue index over the contraction duration.

**Step 6:** Typical healthy biceps at 50% MVC shows a **negative slope** of $-0.5$ to $-1.0\,\text{Hz/min}$ for $f_{med}$. Steeper declines indicate accelerated fatigue (e.g., in COPD, post-polio syndrome, muscular dystrophy).
