<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Chronic Pain Rehabilitation
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Define chronic pain. Explain the Gate Control Theory of pain and its application in **Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS)**.

**Model Answer:**

> **Chronic Pain:** Pain persisting for more than 3 months beyond the expected healing time, often accompanied by psychological distress and functional impairment (IASP definition).
>
> **Gate Control Theory (Melzack & Wall, 1965):** Proposes that a neural "gate" in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord (substantia gelatinosa, Rexed lamina II) can be opened or closed to modulate nociceptive transmission. Large-diameter A-beta fibres carrying non-noxious stimuli (e.g., touch, vibration) **activate inhibitory interneurons** that "close the gate," reducing transmission of pain signals carried by A-delta and C fibres to the thalamus and cortex.
>
> **TENS Application:** High-frequency (50–150 Hz), low-intensity TENS preferentially activates A-beta afferents, closing the gate at the spinal level and providing segmental analgesia. Conversely, low-frequency (2–10 Hz), high-intensity TENS activates descending inhibitory pathways via endorphin release.
>
> **Rehabilitation Significance:** TENS is non-invasive, drug-free, and enables patient self-management, fitting within the biopsychosocial rehab framework. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: VOR and Balance
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the **Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex (VOR)**? State its normal gain value and explain what a reduced gain clinically indicates.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The VOR is a reflex arc that generates compensatory eye movements in the direction opposite to head movement, thereby maintaining stable gaze on the retina during head motion. The arc involves the **vestibular nerve → vestibular nuclei → oculomotor nuclei (CN III, IV, VI) → extraocular muscles**.
>
> **Normal Gain:** $G_{VOR} \approx 1.0$ (eye velocity equals head velocity in magnitude).
>
> **Reduced Gain Indication:** A gain below 0.7 indicates **unilateral or bilateral vestibular hypofunction**, clinically producing **oscillopsia** (apparent movement of the visual world) during head movement and a positive **head-impulse test** with corrective catch-up saccades. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

#### (a) Describe in detail the classification of pathological gaits. With neat diagrams, explain the characteristic features of any three pathological gaits and their rehabilitation strategies. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Pathological Gaits (with characteristic features):**

1. **Hemiplegic (Circumducting) Gait**
   - **Cause:** Upper motor neuron lesion (stroke, TBI, MS) → spasticity, foot drop
   - **Features:** 
     - Hip **circumduction** (semicircular swing) to clear the dropped foot
     - **Plantar flexion** with toe drag
     - Reduced stance time on affected side
     - **Equinovarus** deformity of the ankle
   - **Rehabilitation:** 
     - FES for foot drop (e.g., ActiGait, Bioness L300)
     - Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO)
     - BWSTT, robotic exoskeleton gait training
     - CIMT for upper limb

2. **Parkinsonian (Festinating) Gait**
   - **Cause:** Basal ganglia (substantia nigra) degeneration → dopamine deficiency
   - **Features:**
     - **Short shuffling steps** with reduced stride length
     - **Stooped posture**, flexed hips and knees
     - **Accelerating cadence** (festination) with difficulty initiating movement
     - Reduced arm swing
   - **Rehabilitation:**
     - External **auditory/visual cueing** (metronome, floor stripes)
     - **LSVT BIG** therapy (Lee Silverman Voice Treatment BIG protocol)
     - Balance training, fall prevention

3. **Ataxic (Cerebellar) Gait**
   - **Cause:** Cerebellar lesion (MS, tumor, alcohol-related cerebellar degeneration)
   - **Features:**
     - **Wide base of support**, staggering
     - **Dysmetria** and **dysdiadochokinesia**
     - Irregular, unpredictable step patterns
     - **Truncal ataxia**, titubation
   - **Rehabilitation:**
     - Frenkel's exercises (coordination, balance drills)
     - Visual feedback training
     - Weighted vests or walkers for stability
     - Vestibular rehabilitation if combined

4. **Steppage Gait**
   - **Cause:** Common peroneal nerve palsy → foot drop
   - **Features:** Excessive hip and knee flexion to lift the dropped foot
   - **Rehabilitation:** AFO, peroneal nerve stimulation, strengthening

5. **Antalgic Gait**
   - **Cause:** Pain in lower limb (arthritis, fracture)
   - **Features:** Shortened stance phase on affected side
   - **Rehabilitation:** Pain management, joint protection, gait retraining

(7 marks: 3 for classification + 4 for three detailed gaits with rehab)

---

#### (b) Define stroke. Discuss the Brunnstrom's stages of motor recovery and explain the role of neuroplasticity in post-stroke rehabilitation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Stroke (WHO):** A clinical syndrome of rapidly developing focal (or global) cerebral deficit lasting **more than 24 hours** (or leading to death) with no apparent cause other than vascular origin. Two main types:
- **Ischemic (~85%):** Thrombotic or embolic occlusion
- **Hemorrhagic (~15%):** Vessel rupture

**Brunnstrom's Stages of Motor Recovery:**

| Stage | Characteristics |
|-------|-----------------|
| **1. Flaccidity** | No voluntary movement, hypotonia, areflexia |
| **2. Spasticity appears** | Basic flexor/extensor synergy patterns develop, hyperreflexia |
| **3. Increased spasticity** | Voluntary movement within synergy patterns, no voluntary out-of-synergy control |
| **4. Decreasing spasticity** | Some movement combinations out of synergy, e.g., reaching behind body |
| **5. Complex movements** | More out-of-synergy control, increased coordination |
| **6. Spasticity disappears** | Near-normal isolated joint movements, improved coordination |
| **7. Normal function** | Full restoration of normal motor function |

**Role of Neuroplasticity in Stroke Rehabilitation:**

1. **Hebbian Plasticity** — "Neurons that fire together, wire together." Repetitive, task-specific practice strengthens synaptic connections in surviving cortical circuits.

2. **Use-Dependent Reorganization** — Functional MRI studies show that the **contralesional motor cortex** and **peri-infarct regions** expand their representational maps to take over the function of the damaged area.

3. **Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy (CIMT)** — Forces use of the affected limb by constraining the unaffected limb, driving plastic changes.

4. **Task-Specific Training** — Real-world functional activities (reaching, grasping, walking) produce stronger plastic responses than abstract exercises.

5. **Intensity and Timing** — High-intensity (2–3 hours/day) and early initiation (within 24–72 hours, when stable) yield better functional outcomes.

6. **Robotic-Assisted Therapy** — Provides repetitive, high-dose motor practice (e.g., InMotion ARM, Armeo Power).

7. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — Decode motor intent from cortical signals and provide feedback (e.g., exoskeleton control), promoting cortical reorganization.

(7 marks: 2 for definition + 2 for Brunnstrom stages + 3 for neuroplasticity role)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the structure and function of the vestibular system. Describe the causes, clinical features, and rehabilitation (Epley maneuver) of Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV). (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of the Vestibular System:**

The vestibular system is located in the **inner ear (labyrinth)** and comprises two functional components:

1. **Three Semicircular Canals (SCCs):**
   - Lateral (horizontal), anterior, and posterior canals
   - Oriented in three mutually perpendicular planes
   - Filled with **endolymph** and contain **crista ampullaris** with hair cells
   - Detect **angular (rotational) acceleration** of the head

2. **Two Otolith Organs:**
   - **Utricle** — detects horizontal linear acceleration and head tilt
   - **Saccule** — detects vertical linear acceleration
   - Contain **macula** with hair cells embedded in a gelatinous otolithic membrane
   - **Otoconia** (calcium carbonate crystals) provide inertia

**Central Connections:**
Vestibular nerve (CN VIII) → vestibular nuclei (pons/medulla) → cerebellum, oculomotor nuclei, spinal cord (vestibulospinal tract), thalamus, and cortex.

**Function:**
- Maintains gaze stability via **VOR**
- Maintains postural stability via **vestibulospinal reflexes**
- Provides sense of spatial orientation

**Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV):**

- **Cause:** Dislodged **otoconia** from the utricle migrate into semicircular canals (most commonly the **posterior canal**), creating abnormal endolymph movement with head position changes.
- **Clinical Features:**
  - Brief (< 60 sec) episodes of **vertigo** triggered by head position changes (e.g., rolling in bed, looking up)
  - **Nystagmus** during Dix-Hallpike maneuver
  - No hearing loss (distinguishes from Ménière's disease)
  - Nausea, imbalance

**Rehabilitation — Epley Maneuver (Canalith Repositioning):**
A series of head and body position changes designed to **move displaced otoconia out of the affected semicircular canal** and back into the utricle:

1. Patient sits upright, head turned 45° toward affected side
2. Rapidly laid supine with head extended 20–30° (Dix-Hallpike position), held 30–60 sec
3. Head rotated 90° to opposite side, held 30 sec
4. Patient rolls onto unaffected side, head turned further, held 30 sec
5. Patient sits up slowly

**Effectiveness:** 70–90% success rate in a single session; can be repeated if needed.

(7 marks: 2 for anatomy + 2 for causes/features + 3 for Epley)

---

#### (b) Define a neural prosthesis. With examples, explain how the brain adapts to neural prosthetic devices. Discuss the engineering and biological challenges. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **neural prosthesis** is a biomedical device that interfaces with the nervous system to restore lost sensory, motor, or cognitive function by delivering electrical stimulation or recording neural activity.

**Examples:**
- **Cochlear implant** — restores hearing
- **Retinal prosthesis (Argus II)** — restores partial vision in retinitis pigmentosa
- **Deep Brain Stimulator (DBS)** — treats Parkinson's disease, essential tremor
- **Spinal Cord Stimulator (SCS)** — manages chronic neuropathic pain
- **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** — controls external devices (cursor, robotic arm) via decoded cortical signals
- **Motor Prosthesis / FES** — stimulates paralyzed muscles (e.g., Freehand system)

**Brain Adaptation Mechanisms:**

1. **Cortical Remapping** — The brain's sensory/motor maps reorganize to accommodate the new input source. For example, in cochlear implant users, the **auditory cortex** progressively re-tunes to interpret electrical stimulation patterns as meaningful sound.

2. **Cross-Modal Plasticity** — When one modality is lost, another modality's cortex may be recruited. Blind subjects' visual cortex may process tactile or auditory signals.

3. **Spectral-Temporal Reorganization** — The auditory cortex adapts to the limited spectral resolution of cochlear implants (typically 8–22 channels vs. ~3500 inner hair cells in normal hearing).

4. **Motor Learning for BCIs** — Initial BCI performance is poor; with weeks of training, motor cortex neurons develop a **stable, abstract "cursor-control" representation** that is independent of the original arm-movement code.

5. **Bidirectional Plasticity** — In closed-loop systems, the brain learns to operate the device, and the device's algorithms adapt to brain signals (co-adaptation).

**Engineering Challenges:**
- Biocompatibility of chronic electrodes (glial scarring, signal degradation)
- Power supply, miniaturization, hermetic sealing
- Signal-to-noise ratio in electrophysiological recordings
- Long-term stability of electrode–tissue interface

**Biological Challenges:**
- Variable degree of neuroplasticity between patients
- Cognitive comorbidities affecting learning
- Time-sensitive window of plasticity (children adapt faster)
- Phantom sensations and abnormal feedback

(7 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for examples + 2 for adaptation + 2 for challenges)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DCML and Spinothalamic tracts** — DCML = fine touch, vibration, proprioception; Spinothalamic = pain, temperature, crude touch. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Writing "VOR" without expansion** — Always write "Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex" at first mention.
> 3. **Brunnstrom stages listed incorrectly** — Stage 1 is **flaccidity**, not "spasticity." Reversing these loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing "festinating gait" with "ataxic gait"** — Festinating = Parkinson's; ataxic = cerebellar.
> 5. **Omitting rehabilitation strategies** — Examiner expects "rehab" in every answer, not just pathology.
> 6. **Skipping the "neuroplasticity" angle** — Almost every Module 4 question expects a discussion of plastic adaptation.
> 7. **Not mentioning timing/intensity** — In stroke rehab, "2–3 hours/day, starting early" is a KTU-favored answer point.
> 8. **Forgetting units in numerical questions** — Always state units (m/s, %, kg/m², etc.).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4**

### Chronic Pain
- [x] Pain > 3 months = chronic (IASP definition)
- [x] Gate Control Theory: A-beta fibres close the gate at substantia gelatinosa
- [x] TENS: high-frequency = segmental; low-frequency = endorphin-mediated
- [x] Graded Motor Imagery: laterality → imagined motion → mirror therapy
- [x] Central sensitization = hyperalgesia + allodynia

### Loss of Somatic Sensation
- [x] DCML tract: fine touch, vibration, proprioception (A-beta, A-alpha)
- [x] Spinothalamic tract: pain, temperature, crude touch (A-delta, C fibres)
- [x] Sensory re-education + substitution strategies

### Gait Disorders
- [x] Gait cycle: 60% stance, 40% swing
- [x] Stride length: 1.4–1.8 m; Cadence: 100–120 steps/min
- [x] 5 gaits: hemiplegic, Parkinsonian (festinating), ataxic, steppage, antalgic
- [x] Rehab: BWSTT, FES, AFO, CIMT, robotic exoskeletons
- [x] Symmetry Index > 10% = clinically significant asymmetry

### Balance, Vestibular & Oculomotor
- [x] Three balance inputs: visual, vestibular, somatosensory
- [x] Semicircular canals → angular accel.; Otolith → linear accel.
- [x] VOR gain: $G = \dot{\theta}_{eye} / \dot{\theta}_{head}$, normal ≈ 1.0
- [x] BPPV: otoconia in posterior canal → Epley maneuver
- [x] Three postural strategies: ankle → hip → stepping

### Stroke Rehabilitation
- [x] Stroke = focal deficit > 24 hours (vascular origin)
- [x] Ischemic 85%, Hemorrhagic 15%
- [x] Brunnstrom stages: 1 (flaccid) → 7 (normal)
- [x] Scales: BI (0–100), FMA (0–226), mRS (0–6)
- [x] Neuroplasticity: Hebbian, use-dependent, task-specific, intensive
- [x] 2–3 hours/day active practice; start within 24–72 hours

### Neural Prosthesis & Brain Response
- [x] Examples: cochlear, retinal, DBS, SCS, BCI, FES
- [x] Brain adapts via cortical remapping, cross-modal plasticity, co-adaptation
- [x] Adaptation equation (qualitative): $\Delta M \propto U \cdot S$
- [x] Challenges: biocompatibility, signal stability, patient variability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rehabilitation Robotics — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rehabilitation Robotics** is the branch of biomedical engineering that deals with the design, fabrication, and application of robotic devices and systems for the purpose of assisting, augmenting, or restoring sensorimotor functions in individuals suffering from physical disabilities arising from trauma, stroke, amputation, spinal cord injury (SCI), cerebral palsy, or neurodegenerative disorders.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT834)** classification, rehabilitation robots are categorized into three functional classes:

1. **Assistive Robots** — augment a user's existing capability (e.g., wheelchairs, robotic arms).
2. **Therapeutic Robots** — aid recovery by repetitive task practice (e.g., Lokomat, Armeo).
3. **Prosthetic Robots** — replace a missing limb (e.g., i-limb, Genium knee).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)** loop, the **International Safety Standards (ISO 13482, IEC 80601-2-78)**, and the **biomechanical incorporation** of prostheses with the human body.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suit of Iron Man"

Imagine a child who has had a stroke and cannot lift their arm to drink water. A **rehabilitation robot** is conceptually similar to **Iron Man's suit**: a smart mechanical exoskeleton that not only follows the user's intentions but also *teaches* the muscles to move again through guided practice. The robot is not a replacement; it is a **collaborative partner** that senses, decides, and acts *with* the human.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Payload capacity:** 1.5 kg – 5 kg (upper limb ADL-focused)
> - **Reach workspace:** 0.6 m – 1.2 m (radius)
> - **Repeatability:** ± 0.05 mm (high-precision prosthetics)
> - **Force feedback resolution:** 0.1 N – 1 N
> - **Response latency:** < 20 ms (real-time HRI requirement)
> - **Maximum safe contact force (ISO/TS 15066):** 140 N (quasi-static) on the human body
> - **Biocompatibility grade:** ISO 10993-1 (implantable contact)

## 1.4 Scope of This Module

The KTU Module 4 syllabus bridges **mechatronics** (actuators, sensors, controllers) with **human physiology** (muscle dynamics, neural control). The five pillars covered are:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | ISO 13482, ISO 13849, risk analysis |
| **Interaction** | HRI channels, intent recognition |
| **Manipulation** | ARM design, workspace, grip |
| **Prosthetics** | Upper & lower limb devices |
| **Integration** | Osseointegration, socket design |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rehabilitation Robotics Pipeline (Why & How)

The operational logic of a rehabilitation robot follows a **Sense → Think → Act** closed loop. Let us dissect each stage:

### Stage 1 — Sensing (Perception of Human Intent)

- **How?** Surface EMG (sEMG) electrodes on residual muscles, EEG brain-computer interface (BCI), or inertial measurement units (IMUs) capture voluntary motion intent.
- **Why?** The robot must decode *what the human wants* before it can help. Without intent estimation, the robot becomes a passive exerciser, not an *assistive* device.

### Stage 2 — Control Strategy (Thinking)

Rehabilitation robots typically use one of four control paradigms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Impedance Control** — regulates the dynamic relationship between force and position. Ideal for compliant human-robot contact.
> 2. **Admittance Control** — the human applies force; the robot moves accordingly. Used in assistive arms.
> 3. **Position Control** — rigid trajectory tracking. Used in therapeutic robots.
> 4. **EMG-driven Control** — proportional to voluntary muscle activation. Used in prosthetics.

### Stage 3 — Actuation (Doing)

- **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** — preferred in rehabilitation because they store energy in a spring, providing *intrinsic compliance* and shock absorption.
- **Why?** A rigid actuator striking a fragile human limb can cause injury. Compliance is not optional; it is **biologically mandated**.

## 2.2 International Safety Standards — Why They Exist

Three families of standards govern rehabilitation robots:

| Standard | Scope | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 13482:2014** | Personal care robots (mobile servant, physical assistant) | Defines 3 risk classes (low, limited, high) |
| **ISO/TS 15066:2016** | Collaborative robot safety | Specifies max contact force, speed limits |
| **IEC 80601-2-78:2019** | Medical robots for rehabilitation | Electrical safety, alarm systems |
| **ISO 13849-1:2015** | Safety-related control systems | Performance levels (PL a–e) |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Which standard governs collaborative robot contact force limits?"* — Answer: **ISO/TS 15066** with a **140 N quasi-static limit** on the hand/finger region.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau_h + \tau_r$ | Robot dynamics | Joint torques from human + robot | N·m |
| $Z(s) = \dfrac{F(s)}{X(s)}$ | Mechanical impedance | Ratio of force to velocity | N·s/m |
| $K = \dfrac{\Delta \tau}{\Delta \theta}$ | Joint stiffness | Torque per unit rotation | N·m/rad |
| $f_{max} = 140\,\text{N}$ | ISO/TS 15066 limit | Quasi-static contact force | N |
| $v_{max} = 250\,\text{mm/s}$ | ISO/TS 15066 limit | Max collaborative speed | mm/s |
| $T_r = \dfrac{0.8 \cdot \pi}{BW}$ | Settling time | From control bandwidth | s |
| $PL = f(d, t, S, DC, CCF)$ | Performance level | Risk reduction | categorical |
| $\tau_{SEA} = K_s(\theta_m - \theta_L)$ | SEA torque | Spring deflection × stiffness | N·m |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the equations above, the operator `s` in the Laplace transform domain and the parameter `s` in $T_r = 0.8\pi/BW$ are distinct scalar variables, not a table delimiter.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stroke Rehabilitation:** The Armeo Power (Hocoma) uses **impedance control** to allow patients to "fail safely" while attempting to grasp.
- **Pediatric Therapy:** The InMotion ARM robot delivers **bilaterally symmetric** repetitive training based on motor learning theory.
- **Prosthetic Limbs:** The DEKA LUKE arm has **10 powered degrees of freedom**, controlled via foot-based inertial sensors and EMG.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Impedance Control Law for a Rehabilitation Manipulator

The fundamental goal of impedance control is to enforce a desired *dynamic relationship* between the robot's end-effector position $X$ and the contact force $F_{ext}$ applied by the human. The target relationship is a second-order mass-spring-damper system:

$$
M_d \ddot{X} + B_d \dot{X} + K_d (X - X_d) = F_{ext}
$$

where:
- $M_d$ = desired virtual mass (kg)
- $B_d$ = desired virtual damping (N·s/m)
- $K_d$ = desired virtual stiffness (N/m)
- $X_d$ = equilibrium trajectory

### Step 1 — Express the Robot Dynamics

The actual manipulator obeys the Euler-Lagrange equation:

$$
M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau
$$

The end-effector acceleration in Cartesian space relates to joint acceleration by:

$$
\ddot{X} = J(q)\ddot{q} + \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}
$$

where $J(q)$ is the Jacobian matrix.

### Step 2 — Solve for the Required Joint Acceleration

Invert the kinematic relation:

$$
\ddot{q}_{cmd} = J^{-1}(q)\left[\ddot{X}_{cmd} - \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Desired Impedance Acceleration

From the target impedance equation, the commanded Cartesian acceleration is:

$$
\ddot{X}_{cmd} = \ddot{X}_d + M_d^{-1}\left[F_{ext} - B_d\dot{X} - K_d(X - X_d)\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Required Joint Torque

Using the inverse dynamics relationship:

$$
\tau_{cmd} = M(q)\ddot{q}_{cmd} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) - J^T(q)F_{ext}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

- If $F_{ext} = 0$ (free motion), the robot tracks $X_d$ with a tunable virtual inertia.
- If the environment pushes back (e.g., patient resistance), the robot *yields* proportionally.
- **KTU Insight:** Low $K_d$ and high $B_d$ yield *compliant* behavior — ideal for **spasticity-prone** stroke patients.

## 3.2 Derivation: Settling Time for a Critically Damped SEA

A Series Elastic Actuator with virtual mass $M_s$ and spring stiffness $K_s$ has a natural frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{M_s}}
$$

For a second-order critically damped system, the bandwidth $BW$ and rise time $T_r$ relate as:

$$
T_r = \dfrac{0.8 \cdot \pi}{BW}
$$

The closed-loop bandwidth is bounded by the natural frequency:

$$
BW \le \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}
$$

Therefore, the **maximum achievable rise time** is:

$$
T_r \ge \dfrac{0.8 \cdot \pi}{\omega_n / 2\pi} = \dfrac{1.6 \pi^2}{\omega_n}
$$

> **Numerical Example:** If $K_s = 1000$ N/m and $M_s = 0.1$ kg, then $\omega_n = \sqrt{10000} = 100$ rad/s, giving $T_r \ge 0.158$ s. This is the fundamental speed limit for that SEA.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Therapeutic Robot | Assistive Robot | Prosthetic Robot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Restore function | Augment function | Replace function |
| **Wearer coupling** | External | External | Osseointegration or socket |
| **Control type** | Position/Impedance | Admittance | EMG-driven |
| **Power source** | Mains | Battery | Body + battery |
| **Example** | Lokomat | iARM | i-limb Quantum |
| **ISO class** | IEC 80601-2-78 | ISO 13482 | ISO 13482 + ISO 14708 |
| **Latency demand** | < 100 ms | < 50 ms | < 20 ms |

## 3.4 Derivation of Safe Speed from ISO/TS 15066

The maximum permitted speed for a collaborative robot in contact with a human hand is derived from the **biomechanical pain threshold**:

$$
v_{max} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{B_{eff}}
$$

where $B_{eff}$ is the effective damping of human tissue (~ 2 N·s/mm in the hand).

$$
v_{max} = \dfrac{140\,\text{N}}{2\,\text{N·s/mm}} = 70\,\text{mm/s}
$$

However, the standard conservatively caps this at **250 mm/s** for quasi-static contact, with stricter limits (≤ 30 mm/s) for transient contact scenarios involving sharp surfaces.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rehabilitation Robot Control Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Human User] -->|Intent Signal| B[Sensor Suite]
    B --> B1[Surface EMG Electrodes]
    B --> B2[IMU Gyroscope + Accelerometer]
    B --> B3[Force Torque Sensor]
    B --> B4[EEG Brain Computer Interface]
    B1 --> C[Signal Processing Unit]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Intent Classifier]
    D --> E[High Level Controller]
    E --> F[Impedance or Admittance Controller]
    F --> G[Motor Driver Power Stage]
    G --> H[Series Elastic Actuator]
    H --> I[Robot Manipulator or Prosthesis]
    I -->|Physical Interaction| A
    E -.-> J[Safety Monitor ISO 13482]
    J -.->|Emergency Stop| G
```

## 4.2 Human-Robot Interaction Information Exchange Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    H[Human Operator] -->|Intent / Command| R[Rehabilitation Robot]
    R -->|Feedback / Assistance| H
    H --> E1[Explicit Channel EMG Voice]
    H --> E2[Implicit Channel Gaze Posture]
    R --> F1[Visual Feedback Display]
    R --> F2[Haptic Feedback Vibration]
    R --> F3[Auditory Feedback Beep]
    H --> C1[Cognitive Layer]
    R --> C2[Computational Layer]
    C1 <--> C2
```

## 4.3 Upper Limb Prosthetic Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Socket Interface] --> P[Suspension System Pin or Suction]
    P --> D1[Wrist Rotator]
    D1 --> D2[Terminal Device Multi Articulating Hand]
    D2 --> A1[Thumb Actuator]
    D2 --> A2[Index Finger Actuator]
    D2 --> A3[Middle Finger Actuator]
    C1[Battery Pack] --> M1[Microcontroller]

```

## 4.4 Lower Limb Prosthetic Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Residual Limb] --> SK[Socket with Liner]
    SK --> PY[Pyroelectric Load Sensor]
    SK --> IMU[Inertial Measurement Unit]
    PY --> MC[Microcontroller Knee Controller]
    IMU --> MC
    MC --> MP[Motor Planetary Gear]
    MP --> KN[Microprocessor Knee]
    KN --> AF[Active Foot or Ankle Module]

```

## 4.5 Risk Assessment Flow per ISO 13482

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Identify Hazard] --> B[Estimate Risk Severity]
    B --> C[Estimate Probability]
    C --> D[Compute Risk Level]
    D --> E{Risk Acceptable?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Deploy Robot]
    E -->|No| G[Apply Risk Reduction Measure]
    G --> H[Re-Engineer to PL d or PL e]
    H --> A
```

> **Note on Diagram Choice:** The diagrams above map the *functional and information flow* of rehabilitation robotic systems. Physical drawings of anatomical sockets, gear trains, and EMG electrode placement are conventionally taught via orthotic-prosthetic atlases and are beyond the scope of Mermaid block-level schematics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
**Q:** Define the term *Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)* in the context of rehabilitation robotics. List the **two broad categories** of HRI.

**Model Answer:**
Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) is the multidisciplinary field studying the design, evaluation, and optimization of robotic systems for use by or with humans. In rehabilitation, HRI focuses on **bidirectional information flow** between the patient and the assistive device.

The two broad categories are:
1. **Physical HRI (pHRI)** — involves direct mechanical contact, force exchange, and co-manipulation (e.g., a patient guiding a robotic arm).
2. **Social HRI (sHRI)** — involves cognitive and emotional exchange, often via speech, gaze, gestures, or expressive displays (e.g., the PARO seal robot for dementia therapy).

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: Definition 1m + Two categories with examples 2m]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
**Q:** State **any three** international safety standards relevant to rehabilitation robots.

**Model Answer:**
1. **ISO 13482:2014** — Safety requirements for personal care robots.
2. **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — Guidelines for collaborative robot operation, specifying max contact force (**140 N**) and max speed (**250 mm/s**).
3. **IEC 80601-2-78:2019** — Particular requirements for medical robots used in rehabilitation, including alarm systems and electrical safety.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **ISO 13849-1:2015** — Safety-related parts of control systems, defining Performance Levels PL a through PL e.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark per standard with key feature]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q:** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **architecture of a rehabilitation robotic manipulator (ARM)**. Discuss its **functions, requirements, and challenges** in detail. Also elaborate on the **safety standards** governing its design.

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Functions [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of ARM:** An Assistive Robotic Manipulator is a robot arm mounted on a wheelchair or fixed base, designed to assist individuals with upper-limb disabilities in activities of daily living (ADL) such as eating, drinking, and object retrieval.

2. **Functional Block Architecture (as in Section 4.1):**
   - **Input Stage:** Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-tracking, or sEMG.
   - **Perception Stage:** Cameras for object recognition, force/torque sensors for grasp feedback.
   - **Decision Stage:** Task-level planner (e.g., fetch cup), trajectory generator.
   - **Actuation Stage:** DC motors with harmonic drives, often with series elastic elements.
   - **End-effector Stage:** Multi-finger gripper or specialized tools.

3. **Functions (each 1 mark):**
   - **Object pick-and-place** from table to mouth.
   - **Self-feeding** with utensil attachment.
   - **Door opening and switch operation**.
   - **Computer access** via stylus.
   - **Page turning** for reading.

4. **Requirements (each 1 mark):**
   - **Reach workspace:** 0.6 m to 1.2 m to cover ADL zones.
   - **Payload:** ≥ 1.5 kg (a filled cup weighs ~ 0.4 kg, but a bowl may weigh 1.2 kg).
   - **Intuitive control:** < 30 min training time.
   - **Battery life:** ≥ 8 hours per day.
   - **Safety:** Compliant joints, speed limiting, emergency stop.

#### Part (b) — Challenges and Safety Standards [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Technical Challenges (3 marks):**
   - **Intention ambiguity:** Distinguishing voluntary from involuntary movement (e.g., tremor, spasm).
   - **Variable user capability:** Each patient has unique range of motion, strength, and cognition.
   - **Workspace clutter:** Real homes are not lab environments; the robot must navigate obstacles.

2. **Human Factors Challenges (2 marks):**
   - **Acceptance:** Older adults may distrust robots; cultural factors influence adoption.
   - **Cognitive load:** Complex interfaces can frustrate users with dementia or aphasia.

3. **Safety Standards (2 marks):**
   - **ISO 13482** mandates risk analysis (Hazard, Severity, Probability).
   - **ISO/TS 15066** caps contact force at **140 N** and speed at **250 mm/s** during collaborative operation.
   - **IEC 80601-2-78** specifies electrical and alarm safety for medical-grade devices.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Pattern]

**Q:** Describe the **components of an upper-limb robotic prosthesis** and explain how it is **incorporated with the human body**. Discuss the **challenges and benefits** of lower-limb robotic prostheses in amputee rehabilitation.

#### Part (a) — Upper Limb Prosthesis Components and Body Incorporation [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Socket Interface (2 marks):** The custom-molded socket distributes load across the residual limb. Modern sockets use:
   - **Suction suspension** with a one-way valve and silicone liner.
   - **Pin-lock system** with a lanyard that clicks into a shuttle lock.
   - **Osseointegration** (advanced) where a titanium implant is anchored to the bone, eliminating socket discomfort.

2. **Suspension System (1 mark):** Connects socket to the rest of the prosthesis. Liners, sleeves, or elevated vacuum systems enhance suspension.

3. **Wrist and Terminal Device (2 marks):**
   - **Wrist rotator** (powered or passive) orients the hand.
   - **Terminal device:** Single Degree of Freedom (1-DOF) hook OR multi-articulating hand (e.g., i-limb) with **5 individually powered digits**.

4. **Control System (1 mark):** Microcontroller interprets **sEMG signals** from residual muscles (e.g., flexor digitorum superficialis for hand close, extensor digitorum for hand open). Pattern recognition algorithms decode multiple gestures.

5. **Power Source (1 mark):** Rechargeable **Li-ion battery** integrated into the socket or forearm shell.

#### Part (b) — Lower Limb Robotic Prosthesis: Challenges and Benefits [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Components of a Microprocessor Knee (1.5 marks):**
   - **Load sensor** in the pylon detects stance.
   - **IMU** measures shank angle and angular velocity.
   - **Microcontroller** runs finite-state machine (FSM): Stance Flexion, Stance Extension, Swing Flexion, Swing Extension.
   - **Motor-gear assembly** (e.g., Genium by Ottobock) provides active flexion/extension.

2. **Active Ankle Modules (1.5 marks):** Examples include the **Proprio Foot** (Össur) and **EmPOWER** ankle, which generate push-off energy.

3. **Benefits (2 marks):**
   - **Reduced metabolic cost** of walking by ~ 8–16%.
   - **Trip recovery** through active knee flexion.
   - **Symmetric gait**, reducing secondary musculoskeletal disorders.
   - **Stair descent** with weight-bearing flexion.

4. **Challenges (2 marks):**
   - **Battery life:** Heavy users require daily recharging; flight anxiety is real.
   - **Cost:** Advanced devices (~$50,000–$100,000) are inaccessible in low-resource settings.
   - **Durability:** IP67 rating minimum; exposure to sweat, dust, water.
   - **Socket comfort:** Pressure sores, pistoning, and perspiration remain unsolved.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing ISO 13482 with ISO 10218** — ISO 10218 governs *industrial* robots; ISO 13482 governs *personal care* robots. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the "HRI matics" term** — KTU specifically tests the integrated study of *mathematical models of HRI* (HRI matics). Mentioning only "human factors" without the *control-theoretic* formulation loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Misstating the 140 N limit** — Always specify *quasi-static* contact; transient contact has a different limit (~ 80 N).
> 4. **Skipping the body-incorporation methods** — Socket, suction, pin-lock, **and osseointegration** must all be named for full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring bidirectional feedback** — A prosthesis is not just EMG → actuator. *Sensory feedback* (vibration, electrocutaneous) to the human is equally important.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Rehabilitation Robotics** = Assistive + Therapeutic + Prosthetic.
- **Three Safety Pillars:** ISO 13482 (personal), ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative), IEC 80601-2-78 (medical).
- **Key Constants:** $F_{max} = 140\,\text{N}$, $v_{max} = 250\,\text{mm/s}$ (quasi-static).
- **HRI Categories:** Physical HRI (pHRI) and Social HRI (sHRI).
- **Information Exchange:** Explicit (EMG, voice) and Implicit (gaze, posture).
- **HRI matics** = mathematical modeling of HRI using control theory + biomechanics.
- **Control Strategies:** Impedance, Admittance, Position, EMG-driven.
- **ARM Workspace:** 0.6 m – 1.2 m; Payload ≥ 1.5 kg.
- **Upper Limb Prosthetic:** Socket → Suspension → Wrist → Multi-articulating hand.
- **Lower Limb Prosthetic:** Microprocessor knee + active ankle module.
- **Body Incorporation:** Socket, suction, pin-lock, elevated vacuum, **osseointegration**.
- **Latency Demands:** Prosthetic < 20 ms, Assistive < 50 ms, Therapeutic < 100 ms.
- **SEA Benefit:** Intrinsic compliance, shock absorption, force control precision.
- **Pattern Recognition:** sEMG → features (RMS, MAV) → classifier (LDA, SVM, NN) → gesture.
- **Real Devices to Memorise:** Lokomat, Armeo Power, i-limb Quantum, Genium knee, LUKE arm.
- **Performance Levels (ISO 13849):** PL a (lowest) to PL e (highest); medical robots typically PL d.
- **Biocompatibility Standard:** ISO 10993-1 for any material in contact with skin/tissue.

> **Final Tip:** When answering KTU questions, always *label* your diagrams with sensor, controller, and actuator stages. Examiners allocate **2 of 14 marks** specifically to neat, labeled block diagrams.
